title: Maize Production and Market Development in Eastern Uganda
summary: Government initiatives are focusing on enhancing maize production, quality, and competitiveness through capacity building of advisors and extension workers.
full_content:
--- Page 2 ---
PREFACE
Government of Uganda, through Ministry of Agriculture, Animal Industry and Fisheries (MAAIF) has been implementing agricultural sector development and strategy investment plan that prioritized Maize as important strategic crop to address food security and household income in Uganda. Farm Africa, Alliance for Green Revolution in Africa (AGRA) and other collaborating partners including District Local Governments, Japan International Cooperation Agency (JICA) etc. are strengthening market preferred and nutrient dense and climate smart varieties in Eastern Uganda as well as enhancing Maize production, quality and competitiveness of local Maize.
A key component of the project involves capacity building of the Village Based Advisors (VBAs) and Extension workers in the Maize value chain including farmers and traders. AGRA project will train 100 VBAs and 50 extension workers who will reach 12,000 smallholder farmers in Eastern Uganda.

A key component of the project involves capacity building of the Village Based Advisors (VBAs) and

VBAs and 50 extension workers who will reach 12,000 smallholder farmers in Eastern Uganda.

title: Maize Production Manual for Smallholder Farmers in Eastern Uganda
summary: This Maize manual will be used by advisors and extension workers to help improve maize crop management.
full_content: VBAs and 50 extension workers who will reach 12,000 smallholder farmers in Eastern Uganda. This version contains more photos to help our farmers comprehend more about better Maize farming techniques. The revised version also contains editions such as “Farm record keeping” to assist farmers manage their production costs and enhance their income. It is our wish that farmers, Village Based Advisors and Extension workers best utilize this manual and use practical experiences to provide feedback to better revise the book for future version. title: Maize Production and Market Analysis in Uganda
summary: This section discusses the importance of maize production in Uganda, its market potential, and ecological requirements.
full_content:
--- Page 6 ---
Module One:
Introduction and Background
1.1. Importance of maize
Maize is an important food and income security crop that supports the livelihoods of millions of small-scale farmers in Uganda. Production of maize has increased from 2.8 million MT (2015) to 4 million MT (2017). Its importance is associated with increasing demand and a favourable climate that enables two cropping seasons in a year. Increased demand for poultry products due to rising incomes has further increased demand for maize as animal feed.
This implies that there is a large local potential market for maize. Maize is also a staple food for many people in neighbouring countries, especially Kenya, which is a major importer of Ugandan maize grain. For example, Kenya alone demands an average of 600,000 MT annually and this is likely to rise to 1,000,000 MT, hence increasing demand in the regional market.
1.2. Ecological Requirements

title: Maize Production and Management
summary: This text discusses maize production, its ecological requirements, challenges, and opportunities.
full_content:

1.2. Ecological Requirements
Maize can be grown on a wide variety of soils, but performs best on well-drained, well-aerated, deep,
warm loams. It is well adapted to warm conditions, with an optimum temperature range for plant
growth of 30°C–34°C. Temperatures below 10°C and above 40°C result in poor growth and death of
the maize plant. Maize prefers rainfall of 500 to 600 mm as the optimal range, well distributed over
the growing season.

1.3. Challenges and Opportunities
Maize production, productivity, and quality have stagnated due to several biotic and abiotic factors,
including pests and diseases, declining soil fertility, drought stress, and inadequate extension
services, among others. Average yields have remained as low as 2.2–2.5 MT/ha, compared to a
potential of 8 MT/ha. The quality standards of maize grain produced are generally low, and post-

full_content:

Addressing constraints associated with the maize subsector requires that all value chain actors, from

title: Pre-Planting and Planting Activities for Maize Production
summary: This module covers recommended pre-planting and planting activities for maize production including site selection, land preparation, basal fertiliser application, and planting.
full_content:
--- Page 7 ---
Module Two:
Pre-Planting and Planting Activities
This module covers recommended pre-planting and planting activities for maize production. These are site selection, land preparation, basal fertiliser application and planting
Maize production requires proper management practices to attain its potential yields. Maize takes 105 to 210 days from planting time to maturity. To attain high yields, there is need to apply Good Agricultural Practices (GAPs). GAPs are a set of practices for crop cultivation and farm management that help farmers to best use resources to achieve high yields while minimising costs of production. GAPs are the foundation for better yields and a profitable maize production venture.
GAPs should be observed at all stages of maize production right from site selection, land preparation, seedbed preparation, seed selection, planting, weed management, soil fertility management, harvesting, postharvest handling and storage.
2.1. Planning at Household Level

title: Maize Production Planning and Site Selection
summary: This text covers household-level planning for maize production and site selection considerations.
full_content:
harvesting, postharvest handling and storage.
2.1. Planning at Household Level
At the household level, it is essential for both the husband, wife and other family members to engage in joint planning and decision-making regarding maize production. This includes the choice of variety, intended use (food or income), acreage, and planting schedule. This collaborative approach fosters shared ownership, strengthens family cohesion, and supports sustainable farm management. Joint planning and inclusive dialogue support households to optimise both food security and income generation, while promoting gender equity in agricultural decision-making.
2.2. Site Selection
Maize production requires a suitable site that will support optimal maize growth through the life cycle of the crop. The recommended site for maize planting should have the following: • Deep fertile well drained and aerated, Loam soils make a ball that breaks on slight pressure sandy loams or loamy soils which are fertile.

title: Site Selection and Soil Testing for Maize Agriculture
summary: This text provides guidelines on site selection for maize cultivation and the importance of soil testing in crop management.
full_content:
--- Page 8 ---
During site selection avoid the following:
• Waterlogged areas because maize does not tolerate waterlogged places.
• Very sandy soils
• Soils that are compacted (clay) and too alkaline
2.3. Soil Testing
It is essential to assess soil health before any soil management operations are implemented.
Constraints such as soil acidity and soil nutrient deficiency can lead to significant reductions in crop yields. The soil pH and nutrients levels can be determined by conventional soil analysis in addition to observation methods of crops growing in the field.
Soil testing is an essential crop management decision-making tool that enables to:
• Determine acidity level (soil pH)
• Identify any soil nutrient deficiencies
• Estimate fertiliser requirements for target yields
• Estimate the cost of fertiliser needed and the returns
In the field, a soil testing kit can be used and for further analysis,
soil samples should be taken to a recommended laboratory to Taking soil samples

2.4. Land Preparation
Land preparation involves; bush clearing, removal of tree stumps, termite mounds, and
ploughing. Maize requires warm and moist soil, well supplied with air, and fine enough to allow
rapid germination of the seeds, proper growth of roots to absorb the available soil nutrients. Well
prepared seedbed reduces on the number of weeding times.
2.4.1 Timing of land preparation
The timing of land preparation is extremely important. Land preparation
should begin either at the end of the harvesting period or at least three
weeks (21 days) before planting to allow breakdown of organic matter.
Land should be ploughed at least twice to obtain a fairly rough seedbed.
The farmer should avoid excessive tillage as it damages the soil structure

title: Effects of Tillage and Bush Management on Soil Health
summary: This text discusses the importance of avoiding excessive tillage to protect soil structure and recommends bush management techniques for improved soil health.
full_content: The farmer should avoid excessive tillage as it damages the soil structure (clods) leading to soil erosion. If the site is very bushy, first clear land by slashing down all plants, leave them on the ground, then plough the plant residues into the soil using appropriate equipment. This will help soil to conserve moisture, improve the water-retention capacity, water-infiltration capacity and increase soil Bush Slashing fertility.

title: Land Preparation for Maize Production
summary: Instructions on preparing land for maize production including methods and equipment used.
full_content:
--- Page 9 ---
If the field was previously covered with weeds like Amaranthus spp, which produce a lot of seeds,
then the land needs to be prepared early in the season.
This will encourage most of the weed seeds to germinate as soon as the soil gets any moisture. The
field can then be lightly tilled down or sprayed with non-selective herbicides before maize is planted.
DO NOT BURN PLANT RESIDUES:
This destroys plant nutrients from the residues which
cannot easily be replaced.
2.4.2. Methods of Land Preparation and Related Equipment
Seedbeds for maize production are prepared either using the conventional tillage procedures or
conservation tillage.
1. Conventional tillage:
The farmer uses equipment like hand hoes, animal traction, and conventional and walking tractors
to till land.
a) The hand hoe is the most commonly used equipment by small holder farmers though it is slow
and labour intensive.

title: Conservation Tillage Practices and Management
summary: This text describes conservation tillage practices including no-till/zero till, minimum/reduced till, and ridge (ripper) till methods aimed at minimizing soil disturbance to promote economic and environmental benefits.
full_content:
--- Page 10 --- If ploughing is to be done using oxen or tractor, care
must be taken to work the land when it is dry 2. Conservation Tillage (CT) CT is also referred to as no-till/zero till, minimum /reduced till, and ridge (ripper) till. It is an agricultural
management approach that aims to minimise the frequency or intensity of tillage operations in an
effort to promote certain economic and environmental benefits. The principle of conservation tillage
involves maintenance of at least 30% surface soil cover through retention of crop residues. Retention
of crop residue protects the soil from direct impact of raindrops and sunlight while the minimal soil
disturbance enhances soil biological activities as well as soil air and water movement. Under CT, use of non-selective herbicides is paramount. Depending on the area to be tilled, remove
all tree stumps and other barriers like anthills. If the area is covered with thickets, first slash down the

title: Conservation Tillage Practices and Soil Management
summary: This text discusses conservation tillage practices for soil management including planting depth, weed control, and the use of manure.
full_content: bush, wait for the new germination of weeds and then spray them with herbicides (Round-up, Weed master, Mamba, and Weedall). CT allows timely planting at a reduced cost, improves soil structure, increases water infiltration and soil moisture retention. In addition, it creates more soil organic matter, controls weeds, reduces soil erosion, reduce labour and energy requirements. Under CT, the farmer should adhere to the adequate planting depth since the seed needs more energy to penetrate the sub-soil with its roots and the top soil with its shoot as compared to a ploughed field. Conservation tillage is suitable for all categories of farmers and higher returns are realized if GAPs are followed. Application of manure Compost or animal manure should be added earlier after the first cultivation in land preparation to allow for adequate decomposing. At second ploughing, the manure is churned into the soil prior to planting. Compost manure is applied at a rate of 4 – 6 tons per acre.

Conservation tillage is suitable for all categories of farmers
and higher returns are realized if GAPs are followed.
Application of manure
Compost or animal manure should be added earlier after the first cultivation in land preparation to
allow for adequate decomposing. At second ploughing, the manure is churned into the soil prior to
planting. Compost manure is applied at a rate of 4 – 6 tons per acre.

2.5. Source of Quality Seed
High maize yield begins with good quality seed. Farmers should always be advised to use improved quality seed (Annex 1). The seed should be bought from recognised seed companies or their registered and licensed stockists.

title: Selection of Maize Varieties for Open Pollinated Variety (OPV) Seed in Uganda
summary: This section discusses the selection and management of maize varieties for OPV seed production in Uganda, including criteria for variety selection and methods for harvesting and storing seeds.
full_content:
--- Page 11 ---
Selection of cobs for OPV seed
2.5.1. Choice of Suitable Maize Varieties
There are two categories of maize varieties in Uganda: Open pollinated varieties (OPVs) and hybrids (see annex 1). Improved OPVs are higher yielding than traditional varieties and seed can be recycled for two years by the farmers without a reduction in yield.
Selection should be done before harvesting by marking the best plants with sticks or ribbon. The plants should have big healthy grains, no signs of disease or pest attack on stems, leaves and grains. The plants should be harvested separately, dried and stored for the next season. While in storage check frequently for any signs of pests and disease attack.
Hybrid varieties under good management yield more than OPVs. However, hybrid seed has to be bought every season. Recycling hybrid seed reduces yield potential.
2.5.2. Criteria for Variety Selection
Selection of maize varieties to be grown will depend on the factors listed below:

title: Selection of Maize Varieties for Different Growing Conditions
summary: Factors to consider when selecting maize varieties include adaptability and yield potential, resistance to pests and diseases, and length of growing season.
full_content:
Selection of maize varieties to be grown will depend on the factors listed below:
• Adaptability and yield potential: Maize is produced under a wide diversity of conditions. A farmer should select seed variety that is adaptable to the prevailing production conditions but at the same time gives yields that measure up to the potential of the seed.
• Resistance to Pests and Diseases: A wide range of pests and diseases will attack the maize crop at the different stages of its growth cycle. A farmer should therefore select a variety that can tolerate both pest and disease attack. Several tolerant maize varieties have been developed by NARO (Annex 1).
• Length of Growing Season: The length of the growing season of varieties plays an important role, especially when there is unpredictable variation in the amount and distribution of rainfall. Farmers should use varieties that have early maturity period and drought tolerant

title: Quality Attributes and Germination Test Procedures for Maize Seed
summary: This section covers the attributes of quality maize seed and steps in conducting a germination test.
full_content:
--- Page 12 ---

2.5.3. Attributes of quality maize seed
• Uniformity
• High germination rate > 85%
• Well dried to 13% moisture content
• Purity 98%: Ensure all seeds are of the same variety
• Clean: not mixed with foreign matter like stones or dirt, or other seeds
• Not damaged, broken, shriveled, mouldy, or insect damaged
• Not rotten or discoloured faded
• All non-conformity should not exceed 2%

2.5.4. Steps in Conducting Germination Test
It is important to use seeds that will germinate and give healthy plants; therefore, seeds should be tested for their ability to germinate.

3 Put the seeds in a container with sand. The container should be pierced at the bottom to let water drain
4 Keep the seeds moist, but not wet. Too much or too little water can prevent seed germination
5 After 7 days, carefully dig the seedlings up from the sand, put them on a paper and count seedlings in each category below:

If about 10 seeds (10%) have failed to germinate (90% germination) then use the recommended
seed rate.
If germination is below 90% but above 85%, increase the seeds per acre at planting following
calculation below:
Recommended seed rate = 10 kg per acre
Percentage germination = 85%
Adjusted seed rate = (100÷85) x 10kg = 12kg
The new seed rate will be 12 kg per acre
If germination percentage is below 85%, reject the seed.

title: Maize Planting Guidelines
summary: This text provides guidelines for maize planting including timing, spacing, and management practices.
full_content:
--- Page 13 ---
Always report back to the stockist, extension worker or the seed company (supply)/village-based advisor within 10 days after planting in case of germination failure.
2.6. Planting
Planting should be done on the onset of rains. Early planting takes advantage of the nitrogen flush effect which is the release of accumulated nitrogen in the soil during the dry season.
Normally, the first season of planting is in March and harvesting is in July to August, while the second season is from August to January (Annex 2). Farmers should avoid late planting since it leads to increased incidences of pests and disease attacks hence reduced yields.
Plant in lines (rows) in order to achieve optimum plant population and also ease field operations like weeding, spraying and harvesting.
To ensure proper spacing for a maize monocrop, mark out the field using marked strings at 75 cm for spacing between rows. Place pegs at each 75 cm spot and a string marked at every 60 cm for

summary: This text provides intercropping and planting depth recommendations for maize with legume crops like beans and soybeans in Uganda.

Intercropping
Maize can be intercropped with legume crops such as beans,
soybeans, peas.
Where intercropping is done, adjust the inter-row spacing as
shown below:
• One row of maize with three rows of beans at 120cm
by 60cm and two seeds per hole
• One row of maize with two rows of beans with at a Beans - maize intercrop
spacing 100cm by 25 cm for maize and 50cm by 10cm
for beans
• Two rows of maize with two rows of beans.

2.7. Planting Depth
The speed at which seeds germinate and emerge out of soil depends on planting depth, soil moisture
and temperature. For uniform plant growth, ensure uniform planting depth. For moist soils place seeds
at a depth of 2-3 cm, and for dry planting, place seeds at a depth of 5-10cm (deep seed placement
under dry planting is recommended so that seed germinate only after adequate rains).

title: Ugandan Farming Practices and Jab Planters
summary: This text describes traditional farming methods in Uganda and introduces the Jab planter as an alternative method for planting.
full_content:
Traditionally, farmers in Uganda use hand hoes. For ease of work, farmers should work in teams of three people: one for digging holes, the second for placing fertilisers and covering them with a thin layer of soil, and the third for placing seeds and doing the final covering.

Jab planters: The planter is manually operated, and the advantage is that it digs the hole, places fertiliser, seed and covers it.

title: Basin Planting Techniques for Crops
summary: Instructions on how to create and use planting basins for various crops during the dry season.
full_content:
--- Page 15 ---
Planting basins
Planting basins are small pits in the ground used for planting many types of crops. They are about 15
cm wide, 30–35 cm long, and 15 cm deep – about the size of a man’s foot.
Planting basins can be dug at any time during the dry season, so they are ready for planting at the
beginning of the rainy season.
Basic equipment needed
• A hoe, long string (for measure off the correct distance between the basins e.g 60 cm for two
seeds). Tie knots in the string or clamp bottle tops to the string at the correct intervals with
pliers.
• Two sticks to mark rows and two strong pegs to hold the string at both ends.
Making basins
• Stretch the rope across the field, at right angles to the slope, and fix both ends in place using
the two pegs. Keep the string well clear of the ground so that it stays straight despite any
vegetation. The knots or bottle tops mark where to dig each basin They act as guides for

acre.

title: Weed Management Practices for Maize Crop
summary: This module discusses weed management practices in maize crops including early and late control measures to prevent weed effects on crop quality.
full_content:
--- Page 17 ---
Module Three:
Crop Management Practices
This module focuses on management practices during crop growth and includes weed management, soil fertility management and pests and disease control.
3.1. Weed Management
Effective weed control is a prerequisite for high maize yields. Early control of weeds recommended at 2-3 weeks after planting is extremely important, because the root system of the plant develops at this stage and some weeds secrete chemical inhibitors which limit plant growth. It is also recommended to weed 5-6 weeks after planting to avoid weeds to adversely affect the quality of the crop. Control of weeds can be manually done using hand hoes, hand pulling, mechanical weeders or by chemical means.
3.1.1. Weeds and their effect on maize
Weeds are plants that grow where they are not wanted. It is very important to control weeds in the early stages of crop development to avoid their effects on the crop.
Effects of weeds on maize

title: Effects and Management of Weeds on Maize Crops
summary: This text discusses the effects of weeds on maize crops and provides information on their management.
full_content:
early stages of crop development to avoid their effects on the crop.

Effects of weeds on maize
• Weeds reduce yield by competing with the maize crop for minerals, light and moisture especially during the early stages of crop growth.
• Some weeds are alternative hosts of pests and diseases
• Some weeds are parasitic and poisonous to maize e.g. Striga weeds. A thick growth of weeds in maize makes harvesting difficult.
Maize suffocated by weeds Maize well weeded
3.1.2. Categories of weeds
Annual weeds: These complete their life cycle within one season. In most cases, the seeds produced by annual weeds will germinate very fast and even grow faster than the maize crop itself. The weeds will interfere with the growth of the crop during the critical period of the first three weeks. Annual weeds also produce a lot of seed which can survive and germinate the following season.
14

title: Striga (Witchweed) and Annual Weeds in Maize Gardens
summary: This text discusses the dangers of Striga sp., a parasitic weed that can cause up to 100% yield losses, and other annual weeds in maize gardens.
full_content: --- Page 18 ---The most dangerous weed in maize garden is the Striga sp. Other examples of annual weeds include blackjack, goat weed, wandering jew etc. Striga is a parasitic weed and can cause up to 100% of maize yield losses at farm level. It is common where soil nutrients have been depleted. The weed seeds are normally dispersed by wind, water, man, farm machinery and contaminated crop seeds. Each striga plant can produce up to 500,000 seeds which remain viable in the soil for over 10 years. The seeds normally germinate only in response to chemical stimulants exuded by the host roots. Once germinated, the weed establishes parasitic attachments with the root of the host and starts deriving all nutrients from the host. Perennial weeds: These carry on from one season to another. The weeds persist in maize garden all time every year and reproduce through roots and stems. It is very difficult to control perennial

3.1.3. Methods of weed control
Weed management must be addressed with a holistic approach and begins with correct land
preparation. Identification of the main weed types will determine the correct control methods to use.
The common methods of control include cultural practices, mechanical practices and application of
chemicals using selective herbicides.
Preventative and cultural weed methods refer to any technique that involves maintaining field
conditions such that weeds are less likely to become established and/or increase in numbers. They
include use of clean seed (uncontaminated by weed seeds), site selection, land preparation, timely
planting, soil fertility management, proper spacing, crop rotation, mulching etc.
Manual weed control involves the use of farm tools and equipment like hoes, rakes, fork jembes,

title: Weed Control Methods and Techniques
summary: This text discusses various methods for controlling weeds in agriculture including manual, mechanized, and chemical control.
full_content:
Manual weed control involves the use of farm tools and equipment like hoes, rakes, fork jembes, and pangas, among others. It should be done carefully to avoid damage to the crop.
Mechanised weed control involves use of mechanised farm equipment such as ox-traction weeders and tractor weeders (tines) that remove weeds from the farm. Maize must be planted in rows and weeding should take place at a particular crop growth stage.
Chemical control either speeds up, stops, or changes the weed’s normal growth patterns. This in turn causes the leaves, stems and roots to dry out.
Herbicides are very effective if used properly. Farmers using herbicides need to know the type, the correct dosage and stage to apply the herbicide. All safety precaution measures need to be adhered to and all label instructions strictly followed.
Advantages of chemical weed control
• Herbicides are very effective and take a short time to work.
• Reduces the amount of tillage hence labour saving.

title: Herbicide Use and Management for Weed Control in Maize
summary: This text discusses the proper use of herbicides to control weeds in maize crops, including the importance of selective herbicides and protective measures.
full_content:
--- Page 20 ---
If the incorrect herbicide is used, the consequences could be disastrous. Herbicides are also
expensive and may be a health hazard (poisonous) to the user when not properly used.
Only selective herbicides should be used during weeding. Selective herbicides act against weeds by
killing all other plants and leave only maize to grow. The herbicides are translocated in the plant
system of the affected weeds. Selective herbicides are most effective if applied on weeds when they
are actively growing. Examples include 2,4-D, Tembofrione, Auxo, Maguguma (Annex 3).
Always wear protective clothing when using herbicides. Apply
herbicides at the recommended rates (refer to module 4)
Where Striga is a problem:
• Hand weed regularly (but this is labour intensive) and burn all the weeds.
• Practice crop rotation.
• Intercrop maize with “Striga chaser” Celosia.
• Intercrop with legumes like cowpea and pigeon pea – these can cause suicidal germination
of Striga seed.

title: Improvement and Management of Soil Fertility in Agriculture
summary: This text discusses methods to improve soil fertility for agricultural productivity.
full_content:
• Intercrop with Desmodium to reduce seed bank of Striga in the soil (push and pull).
• Use tolerant varieties e.g Kayongo Go.
• Boost plant health through manure/fertiliser application.
3.2. Improvement and Management of Soil Fertility
Plants need nutrients and water to grow and give good yields. Most of the nutrients come from the
soil and hence soils have to be fertile to sustain plant yields.
Fertile or healthy soil should:
• Be deep enough and well drained.
• Have good structure, texture and well aerated for proper root development.
• Have a favorable soil reaction (i.e. degree of acidity and alkalinity pH range 5.5 to 6.5 for
most crops).
• Have a good supply of both available and reserve plant nutrients.
• Be able to store soluble nutrients.
• Contain sufficient organic matter >2%.
• Support a wide range of micro and macro-organism (fauna and flora).

title: Causes of Soil Nutrient Losses and Soil Infertility
summary: This section discusses the causes of soil nutrient losses and infertility due to various factors such as crop harvest, erosion, destruction of vegetation, and poor soil physical properties.
full_content:
3.2.1. Causes of Soil Nutrient losses/Soil infertility
Nutrient mining is the removing of more essential nutrients (from the soil through crop harvest, soil
erosion, etc.) than what is replaced through addition of organic and inorganic fertilisers. This is caused
by:
a. Loss of soil cover by destruction or removal of crop residues.
b. Accelerated loss of soil organic matter through destruction of vegetation, ploughing, burning of
crop residues, decomposition etc.
c. Poor soil physical properties leading to limited water infiltration and restricted rooting caused by

3.2.2. Essential plant nutrients
Nutrients that are required for the entire plant growth cycle are called essential nutrients. Deficiency of any one of these will make the plant have limited growth, affect flowering and or seed formation.
The essential plant nutrients are divided into two groups: macro nutrients and micronutrients.
• Macronutrients are required by plants in relatively large quantities are nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K), sulfur (S), calcium (Ca), and magnesium (Mg).
• Micronutrients (Trace elements) are required in small (or micro) amounts by plants. They include manganese (Mn), iron (Fe), boron (B), zinc (Zn), copper (Cu), molybdenum (Mo), etc.
3.2.3 Signs of major macro and micronutrient deficiency in maize
Nutrient deficiency in soil can lead to reduced yield in maize. This requires the farmer to routinely

3.3. Soil Fertility Management
Maize is a heavy feeder crop and grows best on fertile soils. Where soils are deficient in nutrients,
the use of fertilisers is encouraged.
Soil fertility can be improved through use of:
• Organic manure
• Inorganic fertiliser
• Other soil amendment practices.

title: Organic Manure and Its Application
summary: This section discusses organic manure types and their application in soil management.
full_content:
--- Page 23 ---

3.3.1. Organic Manure

This refers to decaying organic matter or humus derived from plant and animal residues. They include farmyard manure, compost manure, green manure and organic mulches. Organic fertilisers maintain soil structure, improve water holding capacity and improve aeration in addition to providing the majority of nutrients for plant growth.

Application of organic manure
In one acre of well ploughed, use 4 to 6 tonnes. Organic manure should be applied during ploughing but should be well decomposed two to three months, and planting is done after two to three weeks from the date of application.

Compost manure
This is where crop residues and organic household waste is left to decompose for two to three months, after which the compost is ready for use.

The compositing process
Requirement: dry plant materials, water, ash, green plant materials, animal droppings and topsoil. Steps in a three –pit compost

The compositing process
Requirement: dry plant materials, water, ash, green plant materials, animal droppings and topsoil.
Steps in a three –pit compost

title: Steps for Three-Pit Composting Process
summary: Instructions and steps for creating a three-pit compost system including base preparation, layering materials, turning the heap, and monitoring its progress.
full_content:
Steps in a three –pit compost
Making the base: Find a shady area, dig a pit for the compost and make a bed with twigs or stalks
Chop the materials and heap the layers; sprinkle water to help the heap rot; add animal droppings from chicken, goat cow; add topsoil for insects and worms; add green plant material; sprinkle with ash for Potassium, and water. Repeat the layers 3 or 4 times and cover with soil and dry grass to keep the compost moist.
After 3 weeks, turn the heap layer by layer (this helps the rotting process). After another 3 weeks, the compost will be ready.
Note: Use a temperature stick to monitor the rotting of the heap. Composting manure is applicable for smallholder farmers.
Compost heap illustration using wooden box design

title: Application and Benefits of Compost Manure and Inorganic Fertilizers
summary: This text discusses the application and benefits of compost manure and inorganic fertilizers for agricultural use.
full_content:
--- Page 24 ---

Application of compost manure
Using a small container to a size of 3 spades, or a half basin, distribute the well decomposed
manure in the field evenly and hallow/dig it up, so that it mixes well into the soil and plant after 2 to 3
weeks

Benefits of compositing
• It is cheap because of the readily available raw materials.
• It improves soil texture, water holding capacity and nutrient up-take.
• Gradually release of nutrients for a period of time.

3.3.2. Inorganic Fertilisers
These are manufactured fertilisers that rapidly provide or release nutrients to the plant. Inorganic
fertilisers supply specific nutrients that are lacking in the soil. Examples of inorganic fertilisers include
Di-Ammonium Phosphate (DAP), Urea, Muriate of Potash (MOP), Nitrogen-Phosphorus-Potassium
(NPK), Triple Superphosphate (TSP), and blended fertilisers formulated to meet specific crop
requirements.

title: Fertilizer Application and Soil Nutrient Management
summary: This text discusses the availability, affordability, awareness of farmers regarding soil nutrients, and the 4Rs (Right Source and Right Rate) of fertiliser application.
full_content:
--- Page 25 ---
Availability: This depends on what farmers demand and what the stockists have in stock.
Affordability: The fertiliser should be cost effectiveness, and farmers must be willing to pay for the
fertilisers.
Awareness: Farmers knowledge of soil nutrients availability and need for the nutrients.
The 4Rs of fertiliser application
• The Right Source of nutrients needed by a plant: Matching fertiliser type to crop need e.g.
apPLICATION TSP, DAP, SSP at planting time in the soils which are low in phosphorus to
establish roots and Urea for top-dressing in soils lacking nitrogen.
NOTE: in highly acidic soils, lime should be applied in the appropriate rate to allow uptake of nutrient by the crop.
• At the Right Rate to supply the nutrient quantity needed by the plant: Matching the amount
of fertiliser type to crop needs or growth stage is important. For instance, at planting, plants
require more of phosphorus for root development. The amount to apply should be equivalent

title: Phosphorus Application for Maize Root Development
summary: Information on phosphorus application for maize root development including the amount to apply and timing of fertiliser application.
full_content:
require more of phosphorus for root development. The amount to apply should be equivalent to the amount the crop is likely to remove from the soil. e.g in the case of maize, one ton of the grain (1000 kg) takes from the soil 24.3 kg Nitrogen, 10 kg of phosphorus and 21.14 kg of potassium.
Targeting 3 tonnse/ acre, fertiliser application should aim at
73 kg of nitrogen, 30 kg of phosphorus and 63.42 kg of
potassium assuming the soil had basic fertility.

weeks after germination (30-40 days) and will continue up to 10 weeks. Nitrogen fertiliser needs to be available during these stages.

title: Management Practices for Soil Fertility and Conservation in Agriculture
summary: This text provides information on managing soil fertility through various methods including fertilization practices, minimum tillage techniques, and soil and water conservation measures.
full_content:
• In the Right Place accessed by plant roots (within 10cm in farrows): Keep nutrients where
 crops can use them. Place fertiliser near plants but avoid contact between fertiliser and seed or plant.
Other methods to manage soil fertility include:
• Practice minimum tillage. In a used field, spray with a
 non- selective herbicide (Glyphosate), after 14 days,
 make fallows or hole (basins) and plant with minimal
 disturbance of soil. Moisture can be conserved through
 mulching.
• Soil and water conservation: construct contour bunds
 and terraces on steep slopes, plant cover crops to limit
 loss of water and soil nutrients
Maize under conservation tillage

title: Maize Crop Soil Management and Pest/Disease Control
summary: This section covers soil pH management for maize crops and pest/disease control strategies.
full_content:

• Crop rotation: Maize should be grown in a rotation sequence of maize, legume, root crops and back to maize so as to reduce the risk of depleting the soil of specific nutrients and to
break disease and pest cycles.
• Intercropping: Maize can be intercropped with legumes to manage soil fertility.
• Fallow management: For farmers with adequate land, fallowing or allowing the land to rest for at least 2 years to rejuvenate the soils is advisable. However, in case of inadequate land, a rotation program should be observed to reduce pests and disease accumulation.
Always use the right type of fertilisers at the recommended rate and
using the methods of application after carrying out soil testing.

title: Major Maize Insect Pests and Their Management
summary: This section describes major maize insect pests and their management techniques.
full_content: Maize is attacked by numerous pests and diseases while in the field. The level of pests and disease incidence depends on the presence of the causing organisms, weather, soil conditions, and the relative resistance or susceptibility of the variety. Management techniques to minimise losses due to pests and disease attack include: • Biological • Habitat manipulation • Modification of cultural practices and • Use of resistant varieties • Chemical control (Annex 3) 3.3.1 Major Maize Insect Pests and their Management The major maize insect pests and their management are described in table 4 below.

title: Integrated Pest Management (IPM) Techniques and Methods
summary: This text describes Integrated Pest Management (IPM), which uses various techniques to control pests without relying solely on chemical treatments.
full_content:
system that utilises a combination of all suitable techniques and methods in as compatible a manner
as possible to maintain the pest population at levels below those causing economic injury. IPM is
based on the understanding that no single pest control method will be successful over a long period
of time. Examples of IPM include planting resistant maize varieties, cultural practices such as crop
rotation, timely planting, and biological control. Under IPM, chemicals are used as a last resort.
28

rotation, timely planting, and biological control. Under IPM, chemicals are used as a last resort.

title: Leaf Blight and Ear Rot Diseases
summary: Descriptions and control measures for two common diseases affecting crops: leaf blight and ear rot.
full_content:
[TABLEAUX DÉTECTÉS SUR CETTE PAGE] :
Northern (Turcicum) leaf blight | Small water soaked elongated /oval spots on the lower leaves causing blighting of leaves. At times, this leads to premature withering and leaf shedding leading to reduction in plant growth.
Ear rot | Irregular bleached areas on husks which enlarge until the husks become completely dried, although the plant is still green. Ears appear chaffy, with a white, cottony growth between the kernels. Stem borer injury in the ear often increases incidence of this disease.
• Crop rotation with noncereal crops
• Use resistant varieties
• Bury infected crop residues after immediately harvesting
• Spray with recommended fungicides as soon lesions are noticed
• Timely harvesting is very important
• Reduce the infestation of stem borers and the fall army worm

title: Agrochemicals and Their Use in Crop Production
summary: This section discusses different types of agrochemicals used in crop production and their benefits as well as potential health hazards.
full_content:
--- Page 32 ---
Module Four:
Safe Handling and use of Agro- chemicals
Agro-chemicals are crop protection products used in production and preservation of crops or crop products. Agro-chemicals are becoming an increasingly integral part of crop production.
4.1. Agro-chemicals
4.1.1 Groups of agro-chemicals
• Herbicides (for killing weeds/herbs)
• Insecticides (for insect pests
• Fungicides (for fungal diseases)
• Nematicides (for nematodes)
• Rodenticides (for rodent pests)
• Fertilisers (providing plant nutrients)
4.1.2. Advantages of using agro-chemicals
• Increases food production
• Improves quality of produce
• Decrease costs of production e.g. use of herbicides
• They are labour saving
• Increased profits for farmers
Agro-chemicals are however very harmful if not properly handled, and can cause detrimental health
hazards to the user, consumer and the environment.
Examples of health hazards include nausea, diarrhea, stomachache, nasal bleeding, vomiting, loss

4.2.1. Identification of the pest
The farmer should ensure that they have scouted their field and identified the pest and level of
infestation. In case of any doubt, they should consult an extension worker or take a sample to a trusted
and qualified input stockist or plant clinic.
4.2.2. Buying agro-chemicals
• Always buy chemicals from licensed and registered agro-input dealer shops.
• Read the label on the container for expiry date and any other important messages e.g. active
ingredient.
• Always buy pesticides in their original containers and also make sure the containers are intact.
• Where possible verify whether chemical is not a counterfeit by using Kakasa (e-tag) application.
Note: Always get a receipt from the agro-input dealer indicating date of purchase, name of chemical
and batch number.
4.2.2. Transporting the agro-chemicals
• Ensure that all containers are tightly sealed and the mode
of transportation does not cause any leaks or spillage.

title: Determining Pesticide Dosage and Calibration for Knapsack Sprayers
summary: This section discusses how much pesticide to use in a knapsack sprayer and the importance of calibration based on the label recommendations.
full_content:
--- Page 34 ---
Colour Coding
Class 1a RED - C Extremely Toxic
Class 1b RED - C Highly Toxic
II YELLOW - C Moderately Toxic
III BLUE 293 - C Slightly Toxic
IV GREEN 317 - C Handle with care
4.3.2. Determining how much pesticide to use
The single most asked question in pesticide application is: “How much pesticide (ml) do I put in a
knapsack (20lts)?” The answer is it depends on the calibration of your
sprayer. In short, how many square meters one spray pump full of water
and pesticide will cover.
Always read the label for dilution rate or dosage.
It is important that the amount of pesticide to be used is precise to avoid
excess that could lead to wastage of resources, damage of the crop and
contamination of the environment. When quantity is inadequate, it is likely
that the pest will not be controlled which could also result into pest
resistance.
Always Read the label for recommended
dilution rate or dosage.
Steps of calibrating a knapsack sprayer Knapsack Spray pump

title: Calibrating Knapsack Sprayer for Pesticide Application
summary: Instructions on how to calibrate a knapsack sprayer for pesticide application by measuring the amount of water used per area and determining the correct dosage.
full_content:
dilution rate or dosage.
Steps of calibrating a knapsack sprayer Knapsack Spray pump
Every sprayer has a different capacity, different nozzles with higher or lower output. Also spray,
operaors work at different speeds and pump at a higher or lower pressure. To realise appropriate
application, there is need to calibrate the spray equipment as follows:
• Measure and mark out an area of 10m x 10m = 100sq.m
• Fill the knapsack with known volume of water e.g 15 litres of wate
• Put the knapsack on your back and start pumping, walk at a steady walking pace, spraying
with the nozzle at knee height and recite the word ‘one thousand’ over and over again
making one pump stroke per ‘one thousand’
• Spray the marked area
• After spraying, measure the litre of water that has remained in the spray tank (e.g 10lts
remained)
Amount used to spray area of 100sq.m = 15lts -10lts = 5lts
To work out how much pesticide to measure into the sprayer is now very easy. Look at the

title: Spraying Calculations and Safety Procedures for Agricultural Sprayers
summary: Instructions on calculating water and chemical requirements for agricultural sprayers and safety procedures during mixing operations.
full_content:
--- Page 35 ---
• To calculate how much to measure into your sprayer:
Calculate the volume of water needed to spray an acre
If 5lts covers 100sq.m
An acre = 4000sq.m x 5lts = 200lts
100sq.m
Using Round up at a rate of 1.5lts/acre, calculate the amount of chemical for a knapsack of 20lt
capacity as 200lts of water is needed to dilute 1.5lts of Round up
Therefore, a knapsack will require: 20lts x 1.5lts = 0.15lts of Roundup
200lts
1litre = 1000ml
0.15 x 1000ml = 150mls
A farmer can also calculate the needed mls per litre of water = 150ml/20 = 7.5ml.
Mixing Agro-chemicals
• Mixing and filling operations are the highest risk time for pesticide accidents.
• Read the label carefully and understand the instruction.
• Ensure recommended rates are followed.
• Always mix and fill outdoors to avoid pesticide fumes that can concentrate in a closed area.
• Open pesticide containers with extreme care.
• In case of spillage, wash it off with clean water as soon as possible.

title: Spraying Instructions for Pest Control
summary: Instructions for proper spraying techniques and safety measures during pesticide application.
full_content:
--- Page 36 ---
During Spraying
• Put warning signs in field during spraying to alert the community.
• Do not spray near other people or water sources.
• Spray in the direction of the wind.
• Walk within the rows and direct the nozzles to the targeted pest.
• Preferably spray in the morning hours before 11.00 am or late in the evening after 4.00 pm
bearing in mind when the pest is most active.
• Do not spray when it is about to rain or when it is raining.
• Do not eat, drink or smoke while working with chemicals.
• Minimise talking when spraying.
• Do not touch your face or any other bare skin with soiled hands or gloves.
• Apply the pesticide evenly and in the right amounts.
• Turn off the equipment whenever you pause.
• If your co-worker shows signs of pesticide poisoning, stop the spraying immediately and begin
first aid measures.
After spraying
• Use all the pesticide in the sprayer, at least spray it over adjacent field if it remains

title: Spraying and Disposal Guidelines for Pesticides
summary: This text provides guidelines on proper pesticide use, disposal, and cleaning after spraying.
full_content:
• Use all the pesticide in the sprayer, at least spray it over adjacent field if it remains
• Do not leave pesticide containers at the application site
• Do not re-enter the treated area until after some time (at least 24 hours)
Disposal of empty containers
• Rinse the empty container at least three times and pour the rinse back to the spray pump.
• If metallic, puncture it; if plastic bottle, cut it several times to avoid reuse.
• Select a disposal site away from home and mark it properly 'Container disposal'.
• Disposal of containers following the guidelines on the label.
Cleaning the spray pump and yourself
• While still dressed in protective gear, wash the knapsack and rinse it well to ensure no residues remain.
• Pour all the washing on bare ground.
• Do not wash spray pumps near water sources like rivers, lakes or swamps.
• Remove the protective gear ending with gloves. Wash the protective gear.
• Wash your body thoroughly well with clean water after spraying.

title: Harvesting Practices for Maintaining Maize Quality
summary: This section discusses the importance of proper harvesting and postharvest handling practices to maintain maize quality and prevent losses.
full_content:
--- Page 38 ---
Module Five:
Harvesting and Post-harvest Handling
The Market requires supply of quality maize in desired quantities at desired time. Good quality maize attracts better market price that help farmers earn high income. Many times, farmer experience high losses of their quality maize right from harvesting to marketing. It is estimated that farmers in Uganda lose up to 40% of their produce from harvesting to marketing as a result of poor postharvest handling practices which leads low quality of the maize. It is therefore crucial for farmers to adhere to good practices to maintain the quality of maize during harvesting and postharvest handling.
5.1. Harvesting
5.1.1 Harvesting
This is the process of detaching the maize cob from the mother plant after it has attained full physiological maturity. To ensure quality, harvesting should be carried out on time to avoid food losses and deterioration of quality.
Maize is harvested at different physiological stages depending on the

title: Maize Harvesting Stages and Methods
summary: This text describes different stages of maize harvesting based on its intended use and provides details about physiological maturity.
full_content: Maize is harvested at different physiological stages depending on the intended use. When it is for fresh eating, it is harvested when the cob is green and the grains are beginning to harden. If it is meant for silage making, the whole plant is harvested at milk stage, and when it is meant for grain, it is harvested when it has dried and achieved full physiological maturity. Maize Physiological Maturity: This is the stage when the crop has achieved maximum growth and has the following indicators: • Maize stalk system and cob sheath turn brown. • Ears begin to droop from the stalk and bend downwards depending on the variety. • The grain is hard and has a floury texture when bitten. If a moisture meter is available, the grain moisture content is between 18-24 per cent. Grains form a black layer at kernel tip Drooped ears ready for harvesting 5.1.2. Methods of harvesting Maize can be harvested manually or mechanically. Manual harvesting of maize

meant for grain, it is harvested when it has dried and achieved full Green maize ready for harvesting

between 18-24 per cent.
• Grains form a black layer at kernel tip Drooped ears ready for harvesting

Maize can be harvested manually or mechanically.

title: Maize Harvesting Methods in Uganda
summary: This text describes different methods for harvesting maize in Uganda, including manual and mechanized techniques.
full_content:
--- Page 39 ---
Manual/ Hand harvesting
This is the commonest method in Uganda and is considered practical for crops of under 30 acres. It involves the use of the following techniques:
• Pulling of ears from the stalk of the plant
• Removal of the husks covering the ears
The activity requires 6-10 people per acre per day.
Mechanised harvesting
This is the harvesting of maize using machines (e.g. combine harvesters) and is suited for large commercial farms. Machines simultaneously harvest and remove ears, shells and do partial cleaning of the grain. It has an advantage of ensuring quality, reducing losses in addition to time and labor-saving.
Common practices to avoid during harvesting
Machineharvesting of maize
• Premature/early harvesting: this results into shriveled and rotten maize.
• Throwing cobs on the bare ground and use of dirty containers during harvesting. This increases risks of aflatoxin and other contaminants.

title: Maize Shelling Methods: Hand vs. Mechanical and Motorized
summary: This text describes different methods of shelling maize cobs including hand shelling and various types of mechanical and motorized shellers.
full_content:
--- Page 40 ---
Methods of shelling
Hand shelling: Maize cobs are shelled by hand. It is slow and can be relatively painful on the thumb when large amounts of maize are to be shelled. For farmers using OPV seed, this is the best method since it does not damage the germ and allows for sorting of seed from the best cobs.
Mechanical Shelling: It is carried out using a hand and peddle operated sheller. For the machine to perform optimally, the maize should be dry (13-14% MC). It is a low-capacity tool used by farmers that have very low volumes.
Motorised maize shellers: They are powered by electrical mortars. They are stationed or mobile, imported or fabricated within the country. They can shell between 800kg – 3,000kg per hour. The mobile maize shellers have been designed to ease issues of accessibility. Using motorised shellers reduces postharvest losses and hence more returns to the farmers.

5.2.3. Drying maize
Drying is the systematic reduction of crop moisture down to safe levels for storage. It is one of the key postharvest operations that ensures maize grain quality. During harvesting, the moisture content of grain is between18 to 24 and this should be reduced to 12 to 13% for safe handling. Drying also reduces chances of rotting and germination of the grains.

a) Sun Drying
Cobs are dried in open air on tarpaulin, drying yard, collapsible dryer, drying racks and in cribs. Note: It is recommended to use a maize crib because it protects maize grains from animal attack

title: Maize Drying Practices and Precautions
summary: This text provides information on various maize drying methods and the precautions needed to ensure quality during the process.
full_content:

Drying maize by hanging
Drying maize on a tarpaulin (a tick)

During drying of the grains, take note of the following precautions to ensure quality:
• Turn the grain regularly to hasten and ensure uniformity.
• Keep animals away from the maize to avoid contamination with animal droppings.
• Protect the grain from adverse weather conditions by covering it with tarpaulins or keeping it
under a well- ventilated facility.
• Ensure close monitoring of the moisture content to ensure the recommended standard is
achieved.
• Dry the grains until required moisture content is attained.
Turning maize for uniform drying
b) Mechanical drying
Hot air is blown in the grain to remove excessive moisture under controlled conditions. Hot air is
generated using burning fuel, solar, electricity and biomass.

title: Screen/Sieve and Mechanical Cleaners for Grain Processing
summary: Information on screen/sieves and mechanical cleaners used in grain processing, including their efficiency, limitations, and appropriate applications.
full_content:
--- Page 43 ---
Use of screen/sieve
Common at bulking sites and warehouses. A screen/sieve mounted on a wooden frame is used for cleaning. It is more efficient than using a traditional winnower. One person can clean 500 kgs per hour. Foreign materials and dirt that are smaller than the screen size pass through.
One of its shortcomings is that grains that can pass through the screen have to be sorted from the dirt and this increases the labour and time. On the other hand, foreign material that is bigger than the screen hole size and nonconforming grains have to be removed manually.
A sieve
Mechanical cleaners
These are machines that are operated by engines or mortars and have capacity to clean above 1ton/h with automated sorting system based on the quality parameters like colour, size, and shape. They are appropriate for seed companies and warehouses where big volumes of grain are being handled.

5.3.2. Storage Management
Reception
This is the process of receiving grain into a storage facility and involves the following processes:
• Inspection: Bagged grain received for bulking is checked for physical appearance and any possible signs of non-conformity to grain standards.
• Sampling: small quantities of grain are taken from different bags for analysis (East African Standard 902).
• Testing: analyzing grain samples as per set quality standard
Stacking of produce on pallets
(East African Standard 2).
• Offloading: If the grain meets the set standard, then it is offloaded, weighed and recorded.
• Stacking (bagged): bags are offloaded and placed on pallets in a design that will ensure safety, stocktaking, fumigation and ease of movement within the facility. The stacks of the produce should be 1m away from the wall to allow for inspection. Where produce covers a whole store, a corridor

title: Storage Facility Hygiene Practices
summary: This section outlines general hygiene practices for storage facilities to ensure product quality and safety.
full_content:
--- Page 46 ---
Where hermetic grain storage tank/bag has been used, it should be placed on pallets/dunnage
poles or a wooden platform.
5.3.3. General Hygiene Practices
Health and safety during storage
• Storage facilities/collection centres and surrounding environment should be kept in good
hygienic conditions to ensure quality and safety of the product.
• The store should be cleaned thoroughly before and after keeping in the produce.
• The floor, walls and roof should be cleaned regularly to remove the dust, cobwebs and any other
dirt.
• Bush surrounding the storage facility should be cleared regularly to avoid inhabiting rodents and
other insects.
• Avoid heaping rubbish near the storage facility to keep away rodents and other sources of
contamination.
• Storage facilities should have access to social amenities as per the required standards.
• Storage structures on poles should have rat guards.
• Ensure that the store is closed all the time and is opened as and when required.

• Storage structures on poles should have rat guards.
• Ensure that the store is closed all the time and is opened as and when required.

title: Storage Facility Management and Maintenance Guidelines
summary: Guidelines for managing and maintaining a storage facility to ensure safety and hygiene.
full_content:
• Ensure that the store is closed all the time and is opened as and when required.
• Ventilators and other outlets should have screens that prevent rodents, birds and insects from
entering the store.
• Nonfood materials must not be kept in grain store. They should be kept in a separate nonfood
store.
• Avoid storing maize grain with chemicals like herbicides, fumigants, pesticides, fuel and other
materials that are hazardous (harmful/dangerous) to life.
• Avoid fire in the storage area. It should be a “NO SMOKING” zone. Flammable materials should
not be kept near the food store.
• In case other types of food are kept it the store, each type of produce should be stacked
separately to avoid mixing of produce and to avoid cross contamination.
Repairing the storage facility

Good storage practices

• It is important to inspect the internal and external areas of the store.

signs of contamination.

5.3.4. Storage infrastructure design and maintenance
The following should be taken into consideration to ensure good conditions of maize grain storage:
• The roof should be leak-proof. The floor should be impermeable and easy to clean
• The walls should be easy to clean.
• There should be sufficient ventilation for easy flow of air to remove disorders that may develop during storage and cool the stored maize grain/ produce.
Note: Fans, cyclones and transparent sheets are not recommended in grain storage structures.

Storage records are required for quality control and good handling practices and business management. They include:
• Stack cards (The stack card fixed to a bag stack, used to keep a tally of the number and weight of bags of grain either added or removed from the stack.)

5.4.2 Control of pests
Storage pests can be controlled physical, chemical and biological methods. Improved storage
technologies like the use of hermetic technologies can be used to control pests. Grain infested with
weevils when stored in a hermetic storage the insects die due to suffocation.
Fumigation
This is the application of chemicals/fumigants to control storage pests that attack mainly grains.
Fumigants commonly used include:
• Phosphine • Sulfuryl fluoride
• 1,3-dichloropropene • Formaldehyde
• Chloropicrin • Iodoform
• Methyl isocyanate • Acrylonitrile
• Hydrogen cyanide • Ethylene oxide
Phosphine is the commonly used fumigant for controlling storage insects. However, misuse leads to
development of resistance by some storage insects.

title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Fumigants
summary: This section discusses the advantages and disadvantages of fumigants used in pest control.
full_content:
--- Page 49 ---

Advantages of fumigants
• Toxic to a wide range of pests
• Can penetrate cracks, crevices, wood, and tightly packed areas such as soil or stored grains
• Single treatment usually kills most pests in treated area

Disadvantages of fumigants
• Nonspecific in that they are usually highly toxic to humans and all other living organisms
• Require the use of specialised protective equipment, including respirators specifically approved for use with fumigants

Safety measures during fumigation (see safe use pesticides)
• Keep fumigants safely and out of reach of ordinary persons. Only licensed persons should carry out the fumigation work.
• Wear protective clothing.
• Wash and preferably take bath after fumigation.
• Use a display board indicating the fumigant being used, date of application and person in charge.
• Aerate the grain and stores after uncovering.

title: Mycotoxin Contamination and Control Measures in Agriculture
summary: This text discusses mycotoxins produced by fungi that contaminate grains under poor postharvest handling practices, their effects on human and animal health, and measures to control their contamination.
full_content:
Mycotoxins are poisonous substances produced by fungi which contaminate grains under poor
postharvest handling practices. The common mycotoxins and fumonisms which are known to cause
cancer and growth impairment in children and can cause death if taken un high dozes. Humans and
animals are all affected through consumption of food and feeds containing mycotoxins.
Mycotoxins are resistant to ordinary food preparations and processing such as cooking, making
them a serious public health concern.
Measures to control Mycotoxin Contamination
• Grow varieties (If available) with resistance to mould attack.
• Use recommended post-harvest handling (PHH) practices.
• When available use biological control agent e.g Aflasafe (Types of moulds that naturally prevent
the mycotoxin producing moulds).
• Use of mycotoxin binding agents and deactivators for production of animal feeds.

title: Grain Value Addition and Processing Methods
summary: This section discusses various value addition methods for grains including cleaning, sorting, milling, food fortification, and the products derived from these processes.

Module Six:
Value Addition
Value addition can take a number of forms such as drying, shelling, cleaning, sorting, milling and
fortification depending on the level of the value chain. Value addition is important because of the
following:
• High market prices
• Extended shelf life
• Diversifies grain products hence alternative sources of incomes
6.1. Cleaning and Sorting Maize grains
This is normally done at farm level.
6.2. Milling
Milling is the process through which grains are broken into small pieces for flour. At a higher level,
maize is processed to produce a number of value-added products. The products include maize flour, cereals, snacks, grit, starch and by-products such as maize bran, maize cob meal. The germ of maize
is extracted and used in food and pharmaceutical industries.
6.3. Food Fortification
Fortification is adding vitamins and minerals to foods to improve nutritional composition. The nutrients

title: Grain Fortification and Quality Assurance in Uganda
summary: Regular grain fortification prevents micronutrient malnutrition, strengthens immune systems, and improves cognitive development. Higher milling capacity requires advanced QA/QC systems that include food fortification.
full_content:
regularly used in grain fortification are meant to prevent micronutrient malnutrition, strengthen immune
systems, and improve cognitive development. Food fortification is required because the current milling
of maize grain in Uganda leads to loss of nutrients with the bran.
For millers with higher milling capacity producing over 20 tonnes per day, there is need to set up a
more advanced system of Quality Assurance and Quality Control (QA/QC) that incorporates food
fortification needs. Fortifying maize flour demands that the nutrients (Vitamin A, and Iron), are added
in a manner consistent with national standards (US EAS 2) if it is to be safe and of benefit to the
consumers.

title: Maize Marketing and Market Access in Uganda
summary: This section discusses maize marketing activities including different products and market access points in Uganda.
full_content:
--- Page 51 ---
Module Seven:
Marketing of Maize
A market is an arrangement which facilitates exchange of goods and services. It can also be a place or forum where buyers and sellers meet to perform their transactions.
Marketing consists of all activities involved in moving a product from the point of production to the point of consumption (Farm to the plate). In other words, marketing involves all those activities linking producers and consumers. Effective marketing should ensure that goods are supplied according to the demand i.e. on time and of the quantity and quality that consumers want.
7.1. Maize Products
Maize is mainly marketed as fresh cobs (green maize), grain, flour, cereals, snacks and bran (animal feeds). Maize in Uganda is sold to local, regional and international markets. Local market comprise individuals, institutions (schools, hospitals, prisons, and army) and relief organisations.
7.2. Access to markets

Access to competitive markets requires establishment of both backward and forward linkages between organised farmers (suppliers) and buyers (customers). title: Entrepreneurship and Marketing Strategies for Maize Farmers
summary: This text discusses entrepreneurship skills and marketing strategies specifically tailored to maize farmers.

7.3. Entrepreneurial Skills and Marketing
Entrepreneurship is the capacity and willingness to develop, organise and manage a business venture along with any of its risks in order to make profits. A maize farmer can become an entrepreneur if they come up with technology innovations and adoptions that will help them increase their net profits. It is important therefore that maize farmers acquire good entrepreneurship skills to enable them access better markets.
Marketing of maize follows the 7Ps model (Product, Price, Place, Promotion, Packaging, Positioning and People) and 2Cs (Customers and Competition) as shown in table 7 below.
For maize to be sold to the market, there must be people who want to buy it (the customers), at the same time, sellers must be aware of the competition in the market.
Table 7: The 7Ps of marketing
No. The P Relation to Maize
1 Product Maize
2 Price Price established by both parties.

title: Advantages of Collective Marketing for Smallholder Farmers in Agriculture
summary: This section discusses the benefits of collective marketing for smallholder farmers in agriculture, including attracting large-scale buyers, providing more bargaining power, making farming competitive, reducing costs, and promoting better marketing information.
full_content:
--- Page 53 ---
For smallholder farmers to access competitive markets, they should enter into group
marketing/collective marketing (horizontal linkages) to attain big volumes and have marketing
contracts with big buyers or marketing facilities (vertical linkages) e.g. WFP.
7.4.2. Advantages of collective marketing
• Attracts large-scale buyers such as NGOs, relief agencies and cross-border customers (e.g.
Rwanda, South Sudan) and export markets.
• Provides maize entrepreneurs with more bargaining power (in terms of prices, sales volume,
time of delivery of the maize grain etc.).
• Makes small scale farming competitive since the farmers can access technology, credit,
marketing channels and information while lowering transaction costs.
• Reduces costs if activities are carried out as a group (transporting, grading and packaging of the
maize are costs that can be shared).
• Promotes access to better marketing information and marketing advisory services.

title: Challenges and Benefits of Collective Marketing in Agriculture
summary: Discusses both benefits and challenges associated with collective marketing for agricultural groups.
full_content:
• Promotes access to better marketing information and advisory services.
• Improves quality of products due to timely and sequenced production.
• Enables groups to purchase quality inputs and transport in bulk.
• Provides a link to get training from off-takers, NGO, implementing partners, and government extension.
7.4.3. Challenges of Collective Marketing
Although collective marketing is beneficial to organised groups, the following challenges should be taken into consideration by group members:
• It may be difficult for the group to agree on crucial issues (decision making)
• Dishonesty/non-transparency among members especially the marketing committee may lead to conflicts.
• Poor record keeping may lead to losses.
• It requires safe collection and holding centers in accessible areas, which is not always possible in rural areas. Theft or losses can occur if the bulking facility is in a location that is poorly secured.

• Improves quality of products due to timely and sequenced production.
• Enables groups to purchase quality inputs and transport in bulk.
• Provides a link to get training from the off- takers, NGO, implementing partners and government
extension.

title: Key Success Factors for Group Marketing and Infrastructure Requirements in Rural Areas
summary: This text outlines key success factors for group marketing and the infrastructure requirements needed to attract large-scale buyers to remote/rural areas.
full_content:
• Good road infrastructure is required to attract large scale buyers to remote/rural areas.
• It may require heavy capital investment for assets such as storage facilities and vehicles for transport.
7.4.4. Key Success Factors in Group Marketing
For group marketing to be successful, the following factors are critical:
• Members should be knowledgeable on business management.
• Members should attend group meetings regularly and participate actively in decision making.
• There should be mutual trust among members with emphasis on ethics and integrity.
• The group should have dedicated and committed leaders who are democratically elected.
• The group should have clear and enforceable by-laws on corrupt and unaccountable leadership.
• There should be clearly defined roles and responsibilities especially in relation to promotion and marketing of group products.
• There should be existence of rural finance institutions from which to borrow additional capital funds

grain has been deposited at a particular certified warehouse by a named depositor.

title: Reduction of Postharvest Losses and Commodity Exchanges in Agriculture
summary: This text discusses methods to reduce postharvest losses in agriculture and introduces the concept of commodity exchanges.
full_content:
reducing postharvest losses.
• Enables grain supplier to sell their grain when market conditions and prices are favorable
• Ensures quality grain to the market
• Offers quality assurance services to the grain handlers
• Enables farmers or depositors get access to cash faster against the warehouse receipt
• Eliminates issues of delayed payments to farmers
• Eliminates the need to use title deeds as security for financing grain growers
Commodity Exchange
A commodity exchange is a centralised location or trading platform on which buyers and sellers carry out transactions with or without physical commodities or physical meetings, under a set of clearly defined rules and regulations. Both parties are represented by brokers. The farmer becomes a price maker instead of a price taker. Commodity exchange creates a mechanism for price discovery (true market position price for a product at that particular point in time) to occur in an organised manner

• Commodity exchanges reduce transaction costs such as costs associated with identifying market outlets, physically inspecting product quality, and finding buyers or sellers.
• Improves price discovery (true market position price) through a system of price bidding or an auction and increases transparency.
• Reduces price risks.
• Stake holders are provided with vital information especially on commodity prices through regular publications and dissemination of the information.

title: Contract Farming and Its Advantages
summary: This text discusses contract farming, its mechanism, benefits for smallholder farmers, and how it reduces risks in agricultural production.
full_content:
--- Page 55 ---

Contract farming
In contract farming, agricultural production is carried out according to an agreement between a buyer
and farmers. It establishes conditions for production and marketing of maize for its products for a
specific period of time. Typically, farmers agree to provide agreed quantities and quality of maize grain
according to the set quality standards. In return the buyer commits to purchase the product and, in
some cases, to support production through, for example, the supply of farm inputs, land preparation
and the provision of technical advice.
Advantages of contract farming
• Makes small scale farming competitive: smallholder farmers can access technology, credit,
marketing channels and information while lowering transaction costs.
• Farmers are assured market for their produce at their doorstep, reducing marketing and
transaction costs.
• It reduces the risk of production, price and marketing costs.

title: Financial and Insurance Considerations for Maize Trading
summary: This text discusses financial resources needed for maize trading, the importance of insurance in protecting against agricultural risks, and the need for efficient transport logistics and market information.
full_content:
resources requiring borrowing from financial institutions such as banks, microfinance institutions,
SACCOs, external borrowing, leasing, hire purchase among others.
Insurance: The importance of insurance is to cushion the maize value chain actors against agriculture
risks/natural disasters over which they have limited or no control. Maize commodity for trade should
be insured at all times whether it is in the store or being transported. Warehouses should have full
insurance cover against fire, burglary and other risks.
Transport: Transport is a major component of maize trading and well aligned transport logistics are
necessary for the efficiency of marketing efforts. Transport costs vary depending on the distance
between the farmer and the market, and the mode of transport used.
Market information: Market information is facts and figures that will help farmers, and other value
chain actors involved in the marketing process to make informed decisions and minimise their risks.

title: Market Information and Business Licenses for Farmers
summary: This text discusses how farmers' decision making is improved in terms of production and marketing, as well as the sources of market information and benefits of obtaining a business license.
full_content:
--- Page 56 ---
• Farmers’ decision making is improved in terms of what to produce, how much to produce, the
price to charge, how to promote the product, when and where to sell the product.
• The source of market information includes buyers, farmers’ organisations, fellow farmers, media
(newspapers, magazines, TV, radio), exhibitions (shows, fairs and field days), extension workers,
developmental agencies (NGOs, CBOs, WFP) as well as use ICT including internet messages
and short message services (SMS).
License and taxation
For proper trade, ensure that you pay the business license, local authority taxes (cess taxes) and
sales taxes.
Benefits of a business license
• Legal Protection: A business license protects the owner against being shut down for having an
illegal business.
• Credibility: A license helps reassure customers that they are dealing with a legitimate business.
and not a scammer.
• Financial requisition. To qualify for funds, a business must possess a business license.

title: Maize Grain Standards
summary: This section discusses the importance and specifications of maize grain standards in agriculture.
full_content:
--- Page 57 ---
Module Eight:
Maize Grain Standards
A standard is a document that provides requirements, specifications, guidelines, or characteristics that can be used consistently to ensure that products, materials, processes and services are fit for their purpose.
Standards are developed by national, regional and international standards institutions often to enforce legislation.
These bodies issue specifications for commodities as well as methods of testing.
8.1. Importance of Grain Standards
• Complying with standards reduces post-harvest losses and ensures the final product is of high quality.
• Farmers get better prices while traders and processors get reliable supply of grain that they can sell to their clients.
• Consumers get food that is safe and nutritious to eat.
• Facilitates both national and regional trade
8.2. Specifications
The maize standard requires that maize grain should be free from foreign smell, diseases (moulds),

Immature and shriveled grains, % m/m 1.0 2.0 3.0 vii. Filth, % m/m 0.1 viii. Total defective grains, % m/m 5 9 14 ix. Broken kernels, % m/m 2.0 4.0 6.0 ISO 5223 x. Moisture, % m/m 13.5 ISO 6540

Quality requirement
Current market trends and regulations require safe food to be availed to the market. Consumers of
maize and its products expect to consume safe and good quality maize products. Maize and its
products must meet quality standards.
Farmers should know that maize quality assurance involves prevention of defects from the earliest
stages of cultivation. Therefore, quality of maize grain starts from proper land preparation up to when
maize is sold. It is therefore, important to ensure good practices are followed at all levels
