title: Maize Contaminants Limits and Aflatoxins Regulations
summary: This text outlines the limits of heavy metals in maize as per Codex Alimentarius Commission guidelines and regulations regarding aflatoxin contamination.
full_content: Maize must be free of heavy metals in amounts stipulated by the Codex Alimentarius Commission. Heavy metal quantities can only be determined by testing in a laboratory using a special equipment. The contaminant limits are outlined in 9 below: Table 9: The contaminants limits Heavy Metal Limit Arsenic 0.1 mg/Kg Cadmium 0.1 mg/Kg Lead 0.2 mg/Kg Mercury 0.1 mg/Kg Tin 0.1 mg/Kg 1Mg/Kg is equivalent to ppm (parts per million) Aflatoxins Aflatoxins are poisons released by certain moulds. They come from the soil and air and grow when grain is handled poorly. Aflatoxins are not visible to naked eyes. Determination of presence and level of aflatoxin is done in analytical laboratories and the tests are very expensive. Aflatoxin contaminated maize with levels exceeding 10ppb should not be traded for food or feed. Consumption of high levels of aflatoxin can cause cancer and liver damage and may also lead to death. Always avoid growth of fungus at all stages of the maize value chain.

Maize must be free of heavy metals in amounts stipulated by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.
Heavy metal quantities can only be determined by testing in a laboratory using a special equipment.
The contaminant limits are outlined in 9 below:
Table 9: The contaminants limits
Heavy Metal Limit
Arsenic 0.1 mg/Kg
Cadmium 0.1 mg/Kg
Lead 0.2 mg/Kg
Mercury 0.1 mg/Kg
Tin 0.1 mg/Kg
1Mg/Kg is equivalent to ppm (parts per million)
Aflatoxins
Aflatoxins are poisons released by certain moulds. They come from the soil and air and grow when
grain is handled poorly. Aflatoxins are not visible to naked eyes. Determination of presence and level
of aflatoxin is done in analytical laboratories and the tests are very expensive. Aflatoxin contaminated
maize with levels exceeding 10ppb should not be traded for food or feed.
Consumption of high levels of aflatoxin can cause cancer and liver damage and may also lead to
death. Always avoid growth of fungus at all stages of the maize value chain.

Grain Grading
Grain grading is a process of categorizing grain based on certain quality parameters. This informs
decisions such as storage of the grain, uses of the grain and the purchase price.
Method of Sampling

handling. GHPs along the post-production value chain ensure good quality and indirectly help in
reduction of post-harvest losses through reduction of cross contamination that leads to spoilage.

title: Hygienic Storage and Packaging Standards for Maize in EAS 2
summary: This document outlines the hygienic standards for storing and packaging maize under the EAS 2 Standard, including storage conditions, transport cleanliness, and packaging requirements.
full_content:
Under the provision of the EAS 2 Standard, grain shall be prepared, packed, stored, transported and distributed under hygienic conditions. This implies that maximum care must be taken to ensure that maize is packaged in clean containers by clean people. The areas that the maize is stored should be clean and meet hygiene standards. These stores should be free from pests (e.g. rodents, cockroaches, weevils and other vermin). Modes of transport should also be clean.

8.4. Packaging
Maize, when not handled in bulk, shall be packed in clean bags (maximum 50 kg net weight) or similar acceptable protective containers which will safeguard the hygienic and other qualities of the maize. In East Africa, it is common practice to package maize in second-hand bags. For a maize consignment to comply with the requirements of EAS 2, clean bags must be used.
The containers, including packaging materials, shall be made only from substances that are safe and

title: Container Material and Product Labeling Requirements for Maize Grain
summary: This text outlines requirements for container materials used in maize grain packaging and the labeling information that must be provided.
full_content:
The containers, including packaging materials, shall be made only from substances that are safe and suitable for their intended use. These materials should be food-grade, and, where verification is required, a certificate from the supplier should be available to demonstrate that the materials have been declared safe for use in food and food products.

8.5. Labelling
The following information shall be provided in order to comply with the requirements of EAS 38 and EAS 46:
1. The name of the product should be declared as “White Maize Grain” on the bags. The grade of the maize grain should also be indicated.
2. The name, address and physical location of the producer/packer/importer/distributor should be provided.

title: Commercial Farming Practices and Business Aspects in Agriculture
summary: This section discusses farming as a business, including the principles of improving farm production for profit and sustainability.
full_content:
--- Page 61 ---
Module Nine:
Farming as a Business
A business is a commercial activity designed to supply goods and/ or services that are demanded by
the market with a major aim of making a profit. Farming as a business is built on the principles of
improving farm production to increase profits and/or ensure sustainability of farm output. To make
profit in business, the cost of production must not exceed the income. Farmers need to understand
and update business practices as technology changes.
Farming as a business requires farmers to have entrepreneurship skills that can enable them carry
out farming on a commercial scale.
9.1. Commercial Farming
Farming as a business is based on the commitment of the farmer entrepreneur to carry out farming
as an occupation, with a major aim of making profit. The farmer must ensure proper business
planning, enterprise selection, business record keeping as well as farm enterprise budgeting.

as an occupation, with a major aim of making profit. The farmer must ensure proper business

title: Size and Measurement of Agricultural Land
summary: This text discusses the importance of land size in agricultural planning and its impact on yield forecasting and performance evaluation.
full_content:

For commercial farming to be a viable venture, farmers must accurately know their cost of production,
margins, and understand how to maximise profits by lowering costs of production while increasing
yield.
Acreage
This is the size of land used for agricultural purposes. It is measured in acres or hectares. However,
different communities use different units of measurement for land area.
1 acre = 4,000 square meters
1 hectare = 2.5 acres (10,000 square meters)
The size of a farmland is very important for:
• Planning accurate input requirements
• Forecasting potential yield and income
• Evaluating performance (potential vs actual)
Land is the principal capital for a business enterprise therefore it must be optimally utilised.

title: Productivity and Profitability in Agriculture
summary: This section discusses productivity and profitability in agriculture, including yield per acre and the factors affecting profit.
full_content:
--- Page 62 ---

Productivity
This is the yield per care (kg/acre). As a commercial farmer, it is important to aim at maximum productivity as this determines the total volume of produce supplied to the market. The bigger the volume, the higher the sales and the higher the profits assuming that other factors are held constant.

Profitability
A state of yielding profit or financial gain from a business activity and depends on how the farmer manages costs of production, yield and market price. It is expressed as:

Profit = Sales – Costs of Production

Benefits of farming as a business
Farmers that engage in Farming as a business enjoy the following benefits:
• Growth in income as a result of increased profit margins.
• Improved standards of living due to increased income.
• Diversity of consumed products purchased using increased income.
• Improved nutrition and household food security.
• Increased productivity and efficiency of the family farm.

9.3. Farm Planning and Decision Making
Farm planning is an important aspect of farm business and it involves setting where the business is
going (goals) and how to get there (strategy). A successful farm is not the result of chance or luck,
but the result of good planning. Planning is the process of thinking through what is desired and how
it will be achieved. Plans must be made before any other management activities can be performed.

9.4. Risk Management
Risk refers to the possibility of incurring a loss due to uncertain outcomes. These could be due to
weather, diseases, pests, market, and price. Risk management are the measures put in place by the

title: Risk Management in Agriculture: Mitigating Unpredictable Hazards
summary: Risk management measures are crucial for agricultural success by focusing on manageable risks and reducing the impact of uncontrollable ones.
full_content:
weather, diseases, pests, market, and price. Risk management are the measures put in place by the farmer to avoid or minimise the negative impact of hazards and shocks.
Managing risk is very important for the success of agricultural operations. While some risks can be managed through changes in farming and marketing practices, others cannot be avoided as they are natural (for example, droughts and floods).
Therefore, managers of farm businesses need to focus on managing manageable risks and take measures to reduce the negative impact of those that are uncontrollable.
Maize farming like other businesses is supposed to generate income but this is not always certain. This is because businesses operate in a rapidly changing and unpredictable environment that impacts the outcomes of business activities.

title: Risk Management in Business Environment
summary: While business opportunities exist alongside risks, entrepreneurs must manage these risks as management problems.
full_content:
While the physical, political, economic, social, technological, and trading environment presents
opportunities for business, it also offers threats that make business risky. However, this does not stop
businesses from operating. Entrepreneurs have to expect, accept, and manage risks as they relate
to business.
An identified risk is not a threat but a management problem.

9.5. Farm Record Keeping
This is the documentation of all the farming activities Farm records facilitate quick reference to
previous activities, and this enables the farmer to make quick informed decisions. Record keeping
also provides useful information for assessing the performance of a business at any time.
It is important for farmers to emphasise record keeping to enable them carry out financial analysis
and budgeting as well as making informed business decisions. Farm records include human resource,
finance, production, operation, storage and marketing (Annex 5).

title: Types of Farm Records
summary: This section describes different types of records that are maintained in agricultural operations.
full_content:
--- Page 65 ---

Types of Farm records
(i) Human Resource
These include details of the labour force, leave calendar and profiles of the workers for the farm
(ii) Financial
They include:
• Invoices: document issued by the seller to the buyer demanding payment for the goods and services offered. It indicates the quantity, unit price, taxes and details of the payee.
• Payment vouchers: A document prepared to pay service providers after invoices have been received and verified
• Receipts: It is a document issued acknowledging payment
• Pay in books: Documents indicating money you have paid in the bank
• Cash book Contains information of the money banked, received and spent
(iii) Operation
These are records that contain all farm activities as part of farming as a business. A farmer needs to design a simple comprehensive record entry/report which can easily be understood by all the people on the farm. Records must be easy to understand and written in such a way that they can easily be

(iv) Storage
• Stock card: It is a document in form of a card hanged on a batch of food products or grains indicating the quantity of stock you have at that time. You can also have stock card for all inputs at your farm. Keeping track of stock helps with identifying theft, guarding against wastage and unnecessary purchases and planning for production.
• Stack Card: Card fixed to a bag stack used to keep a tally of the number and weight of bags of grain either added or removed from the stack.
• Goods Received Note (GRN): Document issued out to acknowledge receipt of goods.
• Received Stock ledger books: Records of the stock that has been received in the store/warehouse.
• Outgoing stock ledger books: Records of stock that has been removed from the store.
• Quality control records: Records for quality status of the stored grain.

title: Characteristics and Resource Mobilization for Agricultural Records
summary: This text discusses the characteristics of good agricultural records and resource mobilization in farming activities.
full_content:
--- Page 66 ---

Characteristics of good records
It should be simple and easy to use. If your record keeping system is complicated, it is more likely to generate mistakes.
The financial records maintained should have appropriate level of details depending upon the type of your business. A more complex farm operation requires a more detailed system.
A good system provides essential information in a timely manner. Make sure that your records provide essential information on a timely basis.

9.6. Resource Mobilisation and Management
Resource mobilisation are activities involved in securing required inputs and products for the given programme. It involves making better use of, and maximising existing resources such as land, capital and labour (human) that determine the day-to-day activities and accomplishments. For any given farm activity, it is important to know the resources required and how they can be acquired.

Resource management refers to the decision-making process (setting goal) whereby limited resources (information, land, labour and capital) are allocated to a number of production, marketing, and financing alternatives. Sources of Finance Savings Saving is an act and a habit of putting money away to use at a future date. This can be in the form of cash or material goods whose value appreciates over time. It entails discipline and sacrifice, as one postpones consumption from now to a future date. It leads to capital accumulation over time, which can be invested in profitable enterprises. title: Overview of Loans and Credits for Agricultural Development
summary: This text provides an overview of loans and credits specifically for agricultural development.
full_content:
• Money can be used to expand a business.
• Many organisations that provide a loan will want to see that a farmer has the ability to save before giving him/ her a loan.
• Saving can be used to meet urgent unforeseen emergencies.
• Saving allows a farmer to keep their money safe.

Loan/credit
A loan or credit is money borrowed by an individual from relatives, friends, groups, moneylenders,
micro-finance institutions (MFIs) or banks to meet either social or economic obligations. A loan is
usually paid back over a specified period with or without interest depending on the source and
purpose. Where the loan is acquired for agricultural development, it is referred to as agricultural credit.

title: Pre-requisites and Uses of Agricultural Credit
summary: This section outlines prerequisites for accessing agricultural credit and suggests how it should be used.
full_content:
--- Page 67 ---

Pre-requisites of accessing agricultural credit
• Must be a commercial farmer
• Have production records.
• Have understanding of credit facilities.
• Willingness to pay back
• Must have a business plan

It is advisable that agricultural credit be used to finance:
• Yield -enhancing inputs and technologies e.g. inputs (fertilisers, pesticides, opening up land)
• Cost reducing inputs such as herbicides.
A loan has a cost (interest) that will increase the total cost. Therefore, your yield must increase
significantly to cover this cost and make more profit than a non-borrower.

9.7. Formalisation of a Business
Registration of smallholder farmers is through farmer groups, associations and cooperatives. Large
scale farmers can register as companies and this will require a business name, physical location,
Articles of Association and other relevant documents as required by the law.

9.8. Writing a Business Plan

9.8. Writing a Business Plan

title: Writing a Business Plan for Agriculture
summary: A business plan is a roadmap describing future plans and strategies for a farm, including efficiency improvements, crop planting details, marketing stages, income estimation, and product sales.
full_content:
9.8. Writing a Business Plan
A business plan is written description of a business’ future. It is a road map that describes what you
plan to do and how you plan to do it. It is an important first step for any size of business no matter
how simple a business is. It helps to get organised and make sure all necessary steps are taken and
acts as a guide to help you think carefully about what you want to farm and what to achieve in future.
It is one of the documents that is always required by funders and finance institutions when applying
for a loan or seeking credit.
A simple but good business plan should include:
• The goal, i.e. the direction of the business.
• Plans to make your farm more efficient or more profitable.
• What crops are being planted and what acreage (how much maize s planted).
• The stages of planting to marketing of maize and what activities need to done at each of these
stages.
• Yield and prices used to estimate income.
• How to market your products.

title: Effects and Risks of Climate Change on Maize Production
summary: This section discusses how climate change affects maize production by altering weather patterns and increasing the frequency of extreme weather events.
full_content:
--- Page 71 ---
Module Ten:
Climate Change
Climate change is the shift in the average weather conditions of an area observed over a long period (30 years and above) of time. Climate Change effects are accelerated by human activities such as deforestation, burning of fossil fuels among others. This is mostly evidenced by the overall trend in raising maximum and falling minimum global temperatures. Climate change may affect the maize value chain in various ways depending on the agroecological zones. It reduces the predictability of seasonal weather patterns and increases the frequency and intensity of severe weather events e.g. floods, drought and hailstorms.
10.1. Climate Change Risks
Climate Change risks include negative impact on crop production and farmers’ livelihoods mainly resulting from prolonged dry spells, erratic and excessive rains, storms and lightning. Some regions experience floods while others have prolonged dry spells, pest and disease build up and water

title: Impact of Climate Variability on Maize Yields and Infrastructure
summary: The text discusses various climate-related issues affecting maize production including unpredictable rainfall patterns, prolonged dry spells, floods, and excessive rain causing landslides, leading to poor crop quality and low yields.
full_content:
experience floods while others have prolonged dry spells, pest and disease build up and water shortages which may result into poor quality and low maize yields. Climate variation also contributes to the destruction of infrastructure e.g. road network and markets. All these factors threaten the overall agricultural production value chain.

10.2. Climate Change Impact
Crop production
• Unpredictable rainfall patterns affect planning for field activities such as sowing, weeding, applying inputs especially fertilizers and harvesting. This results into crop failure hence low yields and poor crop quality leading to food insecurity.
• Prolonged dry spell causes water shortages resulting in low yields.
• Prolonged dry spell cause crop pest outbreaks
• Floods destroy maize farms, cause water logging, rotting and increase diseases build up.
• In mountainous areas, excessive rain may cause landslide
raw_english_backup:
experience floods while others have prolonged dry spells, pest and disease build up and water
shortages which may result into poor quality and low maize yields. Climate variation also contributes
to the destruction of infrastructure e.g. road network and markets. All these factors threaten the overall
agricultural production value chain.

title: Adaptation and Mitigation Strategies for Climate Change in Maize Value Chain
summary: This text discusses climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies relevant to the maize value chain.
full_content:
--- Page 72 ---
• Destruction of the infrastructures (road and markets)
• Increase in human diseases e.g. cholera and malaria
• Water shortage
10.3. Adaptation and Mitigation of Climate Change Effects on the Maize Value Chain
There are two approaches to responding to the impact of climate. These include climate change adaptation and mitigation measures.
Climate change adaptation refers to the making of anticipatory or reactive adjustments to prepare for expected climate variability and changing average climate conditions, to moderate harm and exploit beneficial opportunities in agriculture such as climate smart agricultural practices.
Climate change mitigation refers to efforts taken to reduce or prevent emission of Green House Gases that warm the planet. These measures include planting trees, use of environment friendly technologies, and renewable energies (such as solar, biogas etc.)
10.4. Why Adapt to Climate Change in the Maize Value Chain?

title: Maize Production Planning and Record Keeping
summary: This section provides examples of farm planning schedules and production records for maize farming.
full_content:
--- Page 83 ---

Annex 5: Examples of types of records
A. Farm planning schedule:
These are details of the planned farm activities and the tentative dates for carrying them out. The planning schedule should be among the first records a farm manager produces.
An example of a farm planning activity schedule for starting maize production
Activity Timeframe
Buying tools and equipment 1st Month
Preparing land including clearing and ploughing
Marking the field
Digging planting holes
Buying bean seeds and staking materials
Gap filling 2nd- 4th Month
•Routine management (weeding, etc.)
Looking for markets
Harvesting
B. Production records:
These include input records, labour records and records of all other inputs that are used in the production of maize.
(i) Input records
An example of an input record for starting a maize farm
Input Date of Expected useful Unit cost Quantity Total cost
purchase life

Implements (hoes, pangas, etc.)
Seed
Fertilisers
Pesticides
Gumboots (pairs)

title: Start Up Budget and Cash Flow Analysis for Maize Business
summary: This section discusses the expected start-up budget and cash flow analysis necessary for starting a maize business.
full_content:
--- Page 85 ---

Important financial statements
(i) Expected start up Budget:
This helps in projecting the required capital for a business. It can include the capital required for land hire, production, costs of fixed assets and other variable costs required to start your maize business. Can be developed at the start of the business or when planning for some activities. It therefore gives resource requirements for the business.
Format below for preparing a startup budget.
Required resource Amount planned Unit cost Total cost
Land
Labor
Equipment
Planting materials
..................
(ii) Cash flow
The cash flow is an educated guess about when and how much money will come into and go out of your business. The cash flow and sales forecast will enable you to decide what you can afford, when you can afford it and how you will keep your business operating on a month-to-month basis. As part of the business

summary: A cash flow and sales projection will help determine the capital investment needed for an agricultural business idea.
full_content:
plan, a cash flow and sales projection will give you a much better idea of how much capital investment your
business idea needs.
Sample of a cash flow statement
Cash Inflows Amount
Sell of maize grains
Sell of fresh maize cobs
Total Cash Inflows
Cash outflows
Payment for land
Purchase of equipment
Labor expenses
Purchase of stationery
Total cash outflows
(iii) Balance Sheet
The balance sheet describes the assets, liabilities, and equity of a business at a particular point in time. It is
a widely used accounting statement that indicates the economic resources of your organization and the claim
on those resources by creditors. It shows the assets of the business and the debts/liabilities of the business.

Annex 6: Definitions
Goods – Are tangible things or items that are consumable example food, feeds, tools, agro-chemicals
Services – Are activities people perform such as transportation, cleaning, shelling, storage etc.
Value chain – Is a set of linked activities that work to add value to a product. It consists of actors and actions that improve a product while linking commodity producers to processors and markets
Supply – Refers to the total amount of goods or services that are produced
Demand– Refers to the total amount that consumers will consume
Pest– is any unwanted organism (animal, plant, insect, bacteria, virus, fungus)

Preface
I
n the ever-evolving landscape of agriculture, the quest for sustainable, efficient, and
modern production techniques is paramount. The cultivation of Kharif crops, integral
to the agricultural tapestry of many regions, demands a nuanced understanding of
contemporary methodologies that harmonize tradition with innovation.
This book serves as a compendium of modern production technologies tailored specifically
for Kharif crops. It is the culmination of rigorous research, practical insights, and the
collective wisdom of experts in the field. Our aim is not only to impart knowledge but
also to ignite a spark of curiosity and creativity in the minds of farmers, agronomists,
researchers, and enthusiasts alike.
As we delve into the pages of this book, readers will embark on a journey through the
intricate web of Kharif crop production, exploring cutting-edge techniques in seed

summary: This book explores modern irrigation practices for Kharif crops, emphasizing precision and adaptability to diverse conditions.

intricate web of Kharif crop production, exploring cutting-edge techniques in seed
selection, soil management, irrigation practices, pest and disease control, and post-harvest
management. Each chapter is meticulously crafted to provide comprehensive guidance,
backed by scientific principles and real-world experiences.
Furthermore, this book recognizes the dynamic nature of agriculture and the imperative
of adaptability. It acknowledges the diverse agro-climatic conditions, socio-economic
realities, and technological advancements shaping contemporary farming practices. Thus,
while offering a solid foundation in fundamental principles, it also encourages flexibility
and innovation in implementation.

solutions, the integration of modern technologies holds the promise of enhancing

title: About the Editors: GHAZALA BANYALA and TSULTIM PALMO
summary: This section introduces two agricultural professionals from different regions of India.
full_content:
--- Page 9 ---

About the Editors
GHAZALA BANYALA, from Kotputli Rajasthan. My journey in the realm of agriculture began with a Bachelor’s degree in Agriculture from M.B. college of agriculture Tonk, where I delved into the intricate dynamics of the field. Fuelled by curiosity and a thirst for knowledge, I further honed my expertise with a Master’s degree in Agronomy from CPU Kota, Rajasthan. Currently, I am fervently pursuing my Ph.D. in Agronomy from SKNAU, Jobner, Jaipur delving deeper into the nuances of crop production and soil management.

TSULTIM PALMO, from Leh Ladakh (the land of high passes), I have done my schooling from Leh Ladakh after that I did my B.SC (Hons) Agriculture from Sher-e-Kashmir University of Agricultural Science & Technology Jammu (SKUAST-J), and my Master degree and Ph.D. specialization in Agronomy from SKUAST-K. I have qualified ICAR-NET During 2021.

Maize (Zea mays L) is recognized as one of the most adaptable and versatile crops

maize contributes significantly to global grain production, accounting for 36%

35% of the global output, with maize serving as a key driver of the US economy,

title: Maize Production and Economic Impact in Global Agriculture
summary: This text discusses maize production globally, focusing on the US economy and Indian agricultural landscape.
full_content:
35% of the global output, with maize serving as a key driver of the US economy,
achieving remarkably high productivity exceeding 9.6 tons per hectare, double the
global average. In contrast, India, the third-largest producer of maize after rice and
wheat, records an average productivity of 2.43 tons per hectare. Maize holds a cru-
cial position in India’s agricultural landscape, cultivated over 8.7 million hectares
primarily during the Kharif season, covering 80% of the total area. It contributes
around 9% to the national food supply and generates over Rs. 100 billion in ag-
ricultural GDP, providing employment opportunities exceeding 100 million man-
days across farming and downstream agricultural and industrial sectors. Beyond
its role as a staple food and quality animal feed, maize serves as a fundamental raw
material for numerous industrial products spanning starch, oil, protein, alcoholic
beverages, sweeteners, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, textiles, packaging, and paper

title: Modern Crop Production Technology of Kharif Crops: Maize Species and Characteristics
summary: This text provides information on the modern crop production technology for kharif crops, specifically focusing on maize species and their characteristics.
full_content:
--- Page 11 ---

12 / Modern Crop Production Technology of Kharif Crops
Family: Poaceae / Graminae
Zea Mays: 2n = 20 (Annual) only cultivated species
Zea Mexicana: 2n = 20 (Annual)
Zea perennis: 2n = 40 (perennial)
Zea diploperennis: 2n = 40 (perennial)
Botanical Features
A maize crop is a tall, C4 annual plant. It has a height about 4 meters and has an
alternate leaf pattern.
1. Root system: The maize crop has three types of root systems.
A. Seminal roots: these are those roots that are developed from radical.
B. Adventitious roots: the roots that arise from the base of nodes and are
active in nature.
C. Prop roots: these also arise from the base nodes of the stem.
2. Stem: Generally, the internodes are short and thick at the base when they are
tapering. The internode supporting the ear is longitudinally grooved to ensure
the correct alignment of the cob. On the maize crop, the upper leaves play a
crucial role in intercepting light and are significant sources of photosynthesis

title: Maize Flower and Pollen Production
summary: This text discusses the flower structure and pollen production in maize.
full_content:
crucial role in intercepting light and are significant sources of photosynthesis
for grain production.
3. Flower: The inflorescence of male flowers is called tassels, and female
flowers are cobs or ears. The staminated inflorescence, characterized by a
loose panicle, yields pairs of separate spikelets, each comprising both fertile
and sterile florets.
4. Floral Biology: Maize is a monoecious crop that includes both male and
female flowers. Maize is a protandrous crop because the male flower matures
earlier than the female flower. The spikelet contains two functional florets, and
the maturation of both florets is different due to the circumstances. The floret is
surrounded by lemma, palea, 3 anther’s, 2 lodicules, and rudimentary pist. On
average, 2000–7500 pollens are present per anther. tassel with approximately
7000 anthers and 3500 pollens per anther could yield roughly 2.45 crores of
pollen grain. Pollen grains are very small and not visible with naked eyes.

The upper section of the carpel produces a lone ovule without a stalk, comprising a central nucellus surrounded by two rudimentary seed coats known as integuments. As the fused carpels, destined to become fully developed seeds, ascend, they envelope the ovule entirely.

title: Optimal Soil Conditions for Maize Production in Kharif Crops
summary: This section discusses the ideal soil conditions and land preparation practices for growing maize in kharif crops.
full_content:
--- Page 13 ---

14 / Modern Crop Production Technology of Kharif Crops

Soils

Maize can thrive in diverse soil types, spanning from loamy sand to clay loam. Nonetheless, for optimal productivity, it is preferable to choose soils rich in organic matter, possessing a high-water retention capacity, and maintaining a neutral pH. Given maize’s susceptibility to moisture-related stress, especially excessive soil moisture and salinity, it’s advisable to avoid low-lying areas with inadequate drainage and fields with elevated salinity levels. Thus, selecting fields equipped with adequate drainage facilities is recommended for maize cultivation.

Land preparation
a) Soil type: it is sensitive to water logging best grown in well-drained sandy loam soil.
b) Generally maize is grown at first fortnight of week of July.
c) Maize is grown in almost all seasons i.e, kharif, Rabi and spring.
d) It requires deep tillage operation,
Land preparation

title: Maize Planting Methods and Seed Rates
summary: This text describes different methods for planting maize including zero tillage, conventional flat planting, transplanting, and furrow planting, along with their respective seed rates.
full_content:
--- Page 14 ---

15 / Maize
b) Zero – tillage planting: Maize can be grown in zero tillage and primary tillage also. But for it soil moisture should be appropriate at the time of sowing and nutrient should be placed in proper sequence to get good yield and field conditions.
Zero – tillage planting
c) Conventional till flat planting: this method is generally dependent on non-use of herbicides or any chemicals for weed management. Generally, seed-cum-fertilizer planter is used.
Conventional till flat planting
d) Transplanting: This technique is done where there is shortage of land availability, mainly in the case of winter maize as it can cause delay in sowing of the crop due to low temperature. This occurs fondly in December – January.
e) Furrow planting: The main objective of this method is to minimize the evaporation loss, so to grow them in proper furrows for more productivity.

title: Pythium Stalk Rot and Termite Control in Maize
summary: Information on modern crop production technology for kharif crops including pest control and nutrient management for maize.
full_content:
--- Page 15 ---

16 / Modern Crop Production Technology of Kharif Crops
BSMD Apran 35 SD 4.0
Pythium Stalk Rot Captan 2.5
Termite and shoot fly Imidachlorpit 4.0
Nutrient Management
For high yield and good productivity nutrients should be supplied to plant or crop at regular intervals. In maize generally organic nutrients and INM (Integrated nutrient Management) are used, based on soil texture, type, cropping system and plant need and development. Approx 10 t FYM at basal i.e., 15 days prior to sowing along with 150 kg, 70 kg and 70 kg NPK respectively is given. ZnSo4 as micronutrient is also given i.e., 25 kg/ha.
Nitrogen is given in five split doses for good, economic yield of maize.
1st N dose Sowing stage
2nd N dose 4th leaf stage
3rd N dose 8th leaf stage
4th N dose Tasseling stage
5th N dose Grain filling stage
And full doses of P & K are given at basal stage.
For organic nutrient management
i) FYM
ii) Vermicompost @3t/acre.

title: Maize Irrigation and Nutrient Management Practices
summary: This text outlines specific practices for organic nutrient management and irrigation of maize crops.
full_content:
For organic nutrient management
i) FYM
ii) Vermicompost @3t/acre
iii) Straw of summer mung gram is deposited before the sowing of maize.
Water Management
Maize cultivation relies heavily on rainfall for irrigation, especially during the
monsoon season, which accounts for around 80% of maize growth. However, in
areas with reliable irrigation infrastructure, water application should be adjusted
according to rainfall and soil moisture levels. Careful initial irrigation is essential
to prevent water overflow on ridges or beds, with furrow irrigation typically
recommended up to two-thirds of the ridge or bed height. Critical irrigation
stages include tasseling, flowering, and grain filling, necessitating precise water
management. In raised bed systems and under water scarcity, alternate furrow
irrigation can conserve resources. Tied ridges aid rainwater retention in rainfed
regions. For winter maize, protecting crops from cold and frost between December
15th and February 15th is crucial.

title: Maize Weed and Pest Management
summary: This text discusses weed management for maize crops in India, including herbicides used and intercropping options. It also covers insect pest management for the maize crop.
full_content:
--- Page 16 ---

17 / Maize

Weed Management
Cyanodon dactylon, Chloris barbata, Amaranthus viridis and Parthenium hysterophorus are common weeds that occur in the maize crops. Atrazine @ 0.25 kg/ha is used as pre-emergence when the crop is 3 – 5 DAS by knapsack sprayer. Another herbicide used is pendimethalin @ 0.75 kg/ha which is also a pre-emergence herbicide for the maize crop. By intercropping with cowpea can lessen the weed infestation, by mechanical method like use of tractor drawn in between the maize rows to minimize the weed growth is another option.

Crop protection
A. Insect pest management
• Stem Borer:
The primary pest affecting maize in India is the stalk borer, scientifically known as Chilo partellus. This pest is prevalent during the monsoon season and poses a significant threat nationwide. Chilo partellus typically lays eggs on the underside of leaves 10-25 days after germination. Upon hatching, the larvae of the stalk borer enter the whorl of the

Pink Borer, scientifically
known as Sesamia
inference, is a prevalent
pest during the winter
season, particularly in
peninsular India. The moth
of this pest is nocturnal
and deposits its eggs on

after germination. Additionally, Chilo infestation can be managed by releasing
8 Trichocards (containing Trichogramma chilonis) per hectare at the same

title: Shoot Fly Management for Maize Crops in South and North India
summary: This section discusses strategies to manage the shoot fly pest affecting maize crops in both southern and northern regions of India.
full_content:
--- Page 17 ---

18 / Modern Crop Production Technology of Kharif Crops
stage. Intercropping maize with suitable varieties of cowpea presents an eco-friendly approach to reduce Chilo incidence on maize.

• Shoot Fly:
The shoot fly, a significant threat in South India, also impacts spring and summer maize crops in North India, particularly during the seedling stage, where tiny maggots creep beneath the leaf sheaths and sever the central shoot, causing dead heart formation. To combat this pest, it’s crucial to complete sowing before the first week of February to evade shoot fly infestation. Additionally, for spring sowing, seed treatment with Imidacloprid at a rate of 6ml per kilogram of seed is recommended, aiding in the prevention of shoot fly damage and ensuring the health of maize crops.

B. Plant disease management
Throughout India, various diseases afflict maize crops during different seasons, posing a threat to yield if not managed promptly. Major diseases, accounting for

title: Turcicum Leaf Blight (TLB) in Maize Crops
summary: This text discusses the prevalence and management of Turcicum leaf blight (TLB), a major disease affecting maize crops, particularly in specific regions.
full_content:
posing a threat to yield if not managed promptly. Major diseases, accounting for
an estimated 13.2% yield loss, include foliar diseases (5%) and stalk, root, and ear
rots (5%). Turcicum leaf blight (TLB) is prevalent in regions such as Jammu & Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh, Sikkim, West Bengal, Meghalaya, Tripura, Assam, Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Bihar, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Karnataka, and Tamil Nadu.
• Turcicum leaf blight (TLB)
TLB manifests with elongated, grayish-green or tan lesions on leaves, ranging
from 2.5 to 15 cm in length. Management strategies for TLB involve spraying
Zineb or Meneb at a rate of 2.5-4.0 g per liter of water, with 2-4 applications
at 8-10-day intervals. Additionally, it’s essential to plow under crop debris and
cultivate resistant cultivars to mitigate TLB’s impact on maize crops.

title: Maize Lethal Necrosis (MLN) and Polysora Rust in Maize Crops
summary: This text describes the spread, symptoms, control measures for maize lethal necrosis and discusses polysora rust affecting specific Indian states.
full_content:
--- Page 18 ---

19 / Maize
• Maydis leaf blight (MLB)
Maize Lethal Necrosis (MLN) is
widespread across various maize-growing states including Jammu & Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh, Sikkim, Meghalaya, Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, Delhi, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, and Tamil Nadu. It
manifests with lesions on leaves elongated between the veins, measuring 2-6 x 3-22 mm, with limited margins and buff to brown borders. Effective control
measures for MLN involve spraying Dithane Z-75 or Zineb at a rate of 2.4 –
4.0 g per liter of water, with 2-4 applications at 8-10-day intervals after the first appearance of symptoms. Plowing under crop debris and cultivating resistant
cultivars are also recommended strategies to manage MLN in maize crops.

title: Polysora Rust and Banded Leaf and Sheath Blight (BLSB)
summary: This text describes the symptoms, management, and affected regions for two specific plant diseases: Polysora rust and Banded leaf and sheath blight.
full_content:
Karnataka, and Tamil Nadu. The key symptoms include circular to elongated light cinnamon-brown lesions, measuring 0.2-2.0 mm in length, densely scattered on the upper leaf. The uredospore’s exhibit a yellowish to golden coloration. Effective management of Polysora rust entails three sprays of Dithane M-45 at a rate of 2-2.5 gm per liter, initiated upon the first appearance of symptoms, with intervals of 15 days between applications. Additionally, it is advisable to utilize resistant varieties to combat this disease effectively.

• Banded leaf and sheath blight (BLSB)
This disease predominantly affects regions such as Jammu and Kashmir,
Himachal Pradesh, Sikkim, Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh,
Delhi, Uttar Pradesh, and Bihar. Upon onset, white lesions develop on both
leaves and sheaths, with characteristic purplish or brown horizontal bands.

title: Control Methods for Maize Seedling Blight Disease
summary: This text describes effective control methods and harvesting procedures for maize crops, including disease prevention and post-harvest processing.
full_content:
--- Page 19 ---
20 / Modern Crop Production Technology of Kharif Crops
Effective control methods include seed treatment with a peat-based formulation of Pseudomonas fluorescence at a rate of 16 g per kilogram of seed or soil application at 7 g per liter of water (via soil drenching), along with foliar spray of Sheethmar (Validamycin) at 2.7 ml per liter of water. Additionally, stripping of the two lower leaves along with the leaf sheath has proven to be an effective control measure for this disease.
Harvesting and Threshing
The maize is harvested when the cops sheath turns golden yellow and dry up the seeds in cop become hard and is ready for harvesting.
Threshing the cops of maize plant are dried under sun and then separation of grains from shank down by winnowing our mechanical treasures The clean, dry Grains are stored in the storage bins or gunny bags, etc.

title: Training and Development for Agricultural Practices
summary: Training plays an important role in changing farmers' behavior by improving their knowledge, skills, and experiences with current climatic requirements and technical needs.
full_content:
--- Page 3 ---
Government of Nepal
Ministry of Agriculture Development
Regional Agriculture Directorate, central development Region
DISTRICT AGRICULTURE DEVELOPMENT OFIICE
Sindhupalchok, Chautara
Acknowledgement
Training Plays important role for the Change in behavior by improvement of knowledge, skill & ability of the farmers. To follow up and enhance more technically about the local methods, techniques, learning & experiences with current climatic requirement & technical needs are our major responsibility. Good Agricultural practices, traditional knowledge, skill & experience as well as research output techniques will be very helpful for successful Agriculture work. Successful Agriculture works refers to increment in the production of cereals, vegetables & cash crops as well as their marketing for raising the income. Management of the problems which arises during the farming like; disease pest occurrence, soil, Agro-materials also falls under the successive Agriculture work.

title: Maize Production Techniques for Rural Farmers and Agriculture Technicians in Nepal
summary: This manual covers maize production techniques to enhance productivity and quality of maize crops, prepared under the JICA project on Rehabilitation & Recovery from Nepal Earthquake (RRNE). It is useful for both rural farmers and agriculture technicians.
full_content: soil, Agro-materials also falls under the successive Agriculture work. Regarding to the emerging context for commercialization, raising income and upliftment on livelihood of farmers by technical enhancement in simple way, we feel very happy by the preparation of this manual with very useful technical knowledge. This manual which is prepared under the JICA project on Rehabilitation & Recovery from Nepal Earthquake (RRNE) not only useful for rural Farmers, but also very useful for every technician related to Agriculture offices for the training material. It has been hoped that this manual, especially addressing the maize production techniques will support for the increment of the productivity, improving in quality maize production. title: Maize Farming Techniques Manual
summary: This manual covers all technical aspects of maize farming including identification of varieties, land preparation, sowing, fertilizer management, pest and disease control, harvesting, storage methods, and Integrated Pest Management (IPM).
full_content:
included in this Maize Farming Techniques Manual; and as it has covered all technical
aspects like identification of maize variety, land preparation, maize sowing, fertilizer
management, importance of different fertilizers, preparation of compost, seed germination testing method, irrigation, weeding and weed control method, identification and management
of pest and disease in maize, crop harvesting method, storage method, IPM method,
preparation of organic pesticide etc., it is believed to be useful to especially farmers,
respective technicians as well as to other readers.
Finally, I would like very much thanks to staff of the JICA Project Team & Good
neighbors Nepal for their hard work on preparation of this manual.

Stover, non-lodging,
stay green character
(Source: National Maize Research Program, Rampur, Chitwan)
Out of the above, Rampur composite is better for Mid- Hill area, which is described below:
Rampur Composite is a variety of
flint corn recommended in the year
1975. The average height of stalk is
210-220 cm and can be harvested in
110 days of sowing. Rampur
Compost is mostly cultivated in mid-
hills, inner-Terai and Terai areas.
The color of its kernel is orange-

title: Selection and Rotation of Maize Cultivation Land
summary: This section discusses the ideal soil conditions for maize cultivation and the importance of crop rotation to reduce disease and pest issues.
full_content:
--- Page 17 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 10
5.3 Selection of land
• Fertile, light loamy and sloppy (or non-water-logged) soil is good for maize cultivation.
5.4 Crop rotation for reducing disease and pest
• For the controlling of disease and pest, same family crop should not be continuously cultivated every year in same plot, and crop rotation should be adopted in every 3 years.
• If farmers cultivate maize every year in the same plot, virus & other soil borne diseases will be increased gradually.
• The crop rotation can be adopted as given below:
(cid:2) First of all, farmers should divide their field into 3 plots.
(cid:2) In the first year, cultivate the maize in 1st plot, cultivate potato in 2nd plot & if possible cultivate legume in 3rd plot.
(cid:2) In 2nd year, continue the same procedure following same time & season but rotate the crops like maize will be in 2nd plot, potato in 3rd plot & legume or vegetable will be in 1st plot.

vegetable will be in 1st plot.

title: Germination Test for Commercial Seed
summary: Instructions on how to conduct a germination test for commercial seed to ensure quality and prevent disease.
full_content:
--- Page 18 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 11
5.5 Germination test of commercial seed from Agro-vet
• If possible, it would be better to use of certified and disease free seed to prevent seed
born disease, otherwise there is risk of low production.
• Among received seeds from market, the warranty tag of the seed bag should be
checked whether that is the right variety or not. After that, the germination test of the
seed should be done.
• To identify the germination capacity of seeds, the following ways can be followed:
(cid:2) Keep sand/ soil in a flexible container or carton box, and remove stones from
sand/ soil.
(cid:2) Take 200 seeds randomly from the seed bag.
(cid:2) Sow the 100 seed in one cartoon box making 10 rows with 10 seeds in each row,
and repeat the same process for another 100 seeds in other carton box, for
simultaneously getting more exact germination rate. During sowing, seed to seed

title: Seed Germination and Sowing Instructions
summary: Instructions for precise seed germination rate measurement and sowing process with details on seedling count and quality assessment.
full_content:
simultaneously getting more exact germination rate. During sowing, seed to seed
 distance should be 2.5 cm. to 3 cm and the depth should be double of the seed size,
 and should provide daily irrigation.
(cid:2) Count seedlings up to 7 days to know the vigor of seed.
(cid:2) If germination rate of seed was found more than 85%, these seed can be used as
seed. But, if it is less than 85% germination, either other quality seed have to be
procured or sow the seed more.

title: Land Preparation and Sowing Techniques for Maize Farming
summary: This section covers land sanitation before maize cultivation and the application of farm yard manure (FYM) in maize farming techniques.
full_content:
--- Page 21 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 14
5.7 Land preparation and sowing of maize
(A) Field sanitation before cultivation
In case residues of earlier crops remain in the field, the larva of the pest & disease of the previous crops stay alive in soil, and attack the new crops as well. Therefore, field should be cleaned after harvesting.
(B) Application of FYM (Farm yard manure)
• Around 50-60 bamboo baskets (DOKO) of well-fermented FYM per Ropani of land should be applied during the land preparation or first plowing (1-2 month earlier of cultivation).
• Use of unfermented FYM (farm yard manure) can increase pest such as white grubs, cut worms and other worms.
• In case, heaps of FYM put in the field for long time, the nutrients in the FYM will be lost and insect may lay eggs in the heaps also. So, it should be better spread the manure and plow the field immediately.
(C) First ploughing
• 1-2 month before planting, during Dec-Jan, first plowing should be done.

Maize Farming Techniques Manual 14
5.7 Land preparation and sowing of maize
(A) Field sanitation before cultivation
In case residues of earlier crops remain in the field, the larva of the pest & disease of
the previous crops stay alive in soil, and attack the new crops as well. Therefore, field
should be cleaned after harvesting.
(B) Application of FYM (Farm yard manure)
• Around 50-60 bamboo baskets (DOKO) of well-fermented FYM per Ropani of
land should be applied during the land preparation or first plowing (1-2 month
earlier of cultivation).
• Use of unfermented FYM (farm yard manure) can increase pest such as white
grubs, cut worms and other worms.
• In case, heaps of FYM put in the field for long time, the nutrients in the FYM will
be lost and insect may lay eggs in the heaps also. So, it should be better spread the
manure and plow the field immediately.
(C) First ploughing
• 1-2 month before planting, during Dec-Jan, first plowing should be done.

title: First Ploughing and Second Plowing for Maize Sowing
summary: Instructions on first ploughing the field with organic fertilizer and second ploughing before maize sowing.
full_content:
(C) First ploughing
• 1-2 month before planting, during Dec-Jan, first plowing should be done.
• Plowing should be done after putting the well fermented FYM (50-60 DOKO / Ropani) as organic fertilizer on the field.
• Plough the field two times, which will make the soil loose, and make easy for aeration and root growth. In the first time, plough the filed 5-8 cm deep and second time 10-15 cm deep should be done.
• In case, there is big size of soil clods present in the field after first plowing, the breaking of those clods should be done manually with the help of spade or other equipment before those clods become hard.
(D) Second ploughing and sowing of maize
• The field should be ploughed second time just before seed sowing in line with the application of basal dose of chemical fertilizers. Apply 2.6 kg of Urea, 3 kg of DAP, 2.5 kg of Potash per Ropani as a basal dose of fertilizer.
• Line sowing has an advantage over broadcasting as it requires less seed, facilitates easy weed control.

easy weed control.

title: Maize Seed Rate and Sowing Method Guide
summary: This guide provides information on maize seed rate based on germination rates and sowing methods.
full_content:

Maize Farming Techniques Manual 17
(II) Amount of seed
In case of germination rate over than 85%, we should apply the seed rate as mentioned in the table below. If the germination rate is below 85%, the amount of seeds sown should be increased.

Source: JICA Project Team
(III) Methods of sowing seeds
• Seeds will be soaked for 1 night in water, and dry it slightly in shadow before sowing.
• Field needs to have adequate moisture (when the condition of the surface of the field soil becomes somewhat brownish, and when we take the soil on palm and tight)

• Seeds should be sown in the spacing of 20-24 cm from seed to seed and 60-68 cm
from line to line. The different range of spacing & plant number per Ropani is
mentioned in the table below:

title: Weeding and Thinning Practices for Maize Crop
summary: Instructions on weeding and thinning practices for maize crops to improve yield and health.
full_content:

• Weeding will help the maize seedlings to relieve from competition with weeds.
(B) Thinning
• After 25-30 days of sowing maize or at 4-5 leaf stage, thinning should be done.
• Thinning means practice of keeping only one healthy seedling removing another seedling from each spot.
• Thinning should be done just after the first weeding at the same time.

title: Maize Farming Techniques Manual: First Top Dressing and Second Weeding
summary: Detailed instructions for maize farming techniques including thinning, first top dressing application, and second weeding.
full_content:

Maize Farming Techniques Manual 20
• Thinning helps to control competition for nutrition, water, and sunlight among the plants. As a result, remained one plant develops well.
(C) Transplanting
• The removed plants during thinning can be transplanted to the missing spot of field.
• In case the missing spot found, the transplanting should be done after enough watering. But, the root of the maize plant should be pulled out with soil in the root; otherwise, there might be problem of no formation of cob, or no kernel on the cob.
(D) First top dressing
• After 25-30 days of sowing, we have to apply urea immediately as first top dressing. Applying of 0.87 kg Urea per Ropani, first weeding should be done 25-30 days after sowing. We should apply quarter tea spoon of urea near (5-10 cm) to each plant, and cover it by soil.

title: Maize Weed Control and Nutritional Management Guide
summary: Instructions for managing weeds and providing proper top dressings and ridge making in maize crops.
full_content:
The weeds should also be removed on knee-height stage as second weeding after 20-25 days of first weeding.
(B) Second top dressing
After 20-25 days of first top dressing, during knee height stage, we should apply the urea as second top dressing near the plant (5-10 cm from plant) after second weeding, like first top dressing.
(C) Making ridge
• After the second weeding and top dressing, each nodal root of maize plant should be covered by soil for the prevention of lodging in plants.
• At the same time of covering, we should make the drainage way between each line of plants, which helps to prevent lodging while it rains heavily.

After 20-25 days of first top dressing, during knee height stage, we should apply the

(C) Making ridge
• After the second weeding and top dressing, each nodal root of maize plant should
be covered by soil for the prevention of lodging in plants.
• At the same time of covering, we should make the drainage way between each
line of plants, which helps to prevent lodging while it rains heavily.

title: Maize Farming Techniques Manual: Third Farm Work and Irrigation
summary: Detailed instructions for maize farming techniques including weeding, top dressing, making ridge, drainage, and irrigation.
full_content:
--- Page 28 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 21
6.3 Third farm work: (25-30 days after second farm work)
(A) Third weeding
The weeds should be removed again on tasselling stage as third weeding after 20-25 days of second weeding applying the urea.
(B) Third top dressing
After 20-25 days of second top dressing, during tasseling stage, we have to apply urea near each plant (5-10 cm) as third top dressing immediately just after 3rd weeding.
(C) Making ridge
After the third weeding and top dressing, each nodal root of maize plant should be covered by soil for the prevention of lodging in plants.
(D) Making drainage
In the fourth farm work, drainage should be made between each line of plants, which will help to prevent root rot problems by plant logging during heavy rain.
7. Irrigation: only in case of availability of irrigation facility
For better maize production, soil must have adequate moisture until 40-45 days from

title: Maize Irrigation and Weed Management Practices
summary: This text provides guidelines for maize production including irrigation needs and weed management strategies.
full_content:
For better maize production, soil must have adequate moisture until 40-45 days from sowing. Likewise, Soil moisture should be maintained also in the tasseling and kernel developing stage. Irrigation is necessary after the first weeding and during the tassel stage if the field goes dry.

8. Weed Management
• If weeding is not done in proper time, the unnecessary weeds and plants compete for nutrients, light, water and place, which obstructs in proper growth of plants and decreases yields.
• Therefore, unnecessary plants and weeds have to be removed in well manner. This results for better production by well growth and development of plant,

if the field goes dry.
8. Weed Management
• If weeding is not done in proper time, the unnecessary weeds and plants compete for
nutrients, light, water and place, which obstructs in proper growth of plants and
decreases yields.
• Therefore, unnecessary plants and weeds have to be removed in well manner. This

title: Major Diseases and Pests in Maize Fields and Their Control Methods
summary: This section discusses major weeds and diseases affecting maize fields along with methods to control them.
full_content:
--- Page 29 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 22
8.1 Major weeds in maize field
a) Commelina grass
b) Digiteria grass
c) Cyperus grass
d) Cynodon grass
e) Ageratum grass
8.2 Methods of controlling weeds
• Plow the maize filed properly earlier to cultivation.
• At time of sowing, remove weeds and cover the maize plant by soil during the time of weeding (20-25 days of sowing).
• The weeds like Cyperus should be uprooted together with the underground roots, and root galls should be buried or burned away from the field.
• If the field is ploughed in summer season, the weeds die due to the sun light.
9. Major Disease, Pests and Methods of Controlling Them
9.1. Major causes of disease and pest attack
• Use of not well-decomposed FYM.
• Residues of previous crops.
• Lack of proper cleaning of the field.
• The fungal disease like Downey mildew can attack, if the temperature and humidity is high.
raw_english_backup:
--- Page 29 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 22
8.1 Major weeds in maize field
a) Commelina grass
b) Digiteria grass
c) Cyperus grass
d) Cynodon grass
e) Ageratum grass
8.2 Methods of controlling weeds
• Plow the maize filed properly earlier to cultivation.
• At time of sowing, remove weeds and cover the maize plant by soil during the time of
weeding (20-25 days of sowing).
• The weeds like Cyperus should be uprooted together with the underground roots, and
root galls should be buried or burned away from the field.
• If the field is ploughed in summer season, the weeds die due to the sun light.
9. Major Disease, Pests and Methods of Controlling Them
9.1. Major causes of disease and pest attack
• Use of not well-decomposed FYM.
• Residues of previous crops.
• Lack of proper cleaning of the field.
• The fungal disease like Downey mildew can attack, if the temperature and humidity is
high.

title: Maize Harvesting and Seed Production Techniques
summary: This document provides detailed instructions on maize harvesting techniques, including the timing of harvest based on maturity stages.
full_content:
--- Page 35 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 28
Fig. No. Maize stored making bundle under the roof
Source: JICA Project Team
11.3 Self-seed production method
(A) Time of harvesting
• The harvesting time starts after the heavy rainfall stop. When the husk becomes dry or
dark brownish color, seed should also be dried on the cob of standing plant in the field,
the maize should be harvested.
• Harvesting should be done in dry or no rainfall day.
• At time of harvesting, a black spot is seen in the bottom (side attached to the cob) of
seed. This black spot suggests it is ready for harvest.
Fig. No. 11.3.1 Pre-maturation stage i.e. not
Fig. No. 11.3.2 Full-maturation stage i.e.
proper time of harvesting for seed production
proper time of harvesting for Maize seed
Source: JICA Project Team

title: Farm Yard Manure (FYM) in Maize Farming Techniques Manual
summary: This manual discusses the importance of using well-decomposed FYM for maize farming to avoid harmful effects and improve soil quality.
full_content:
--- Page 38 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 31
PART 1: Farm Yard Manure (FYM)
1. Introduction
Generally Nepalese farmers have the practice of collecting FYM from their livestock shed as an organic fertilizer for their crop fields.
Farm Yard Manure is the manure prepared in decomposed from by collecting dung and urine of farm animals along with wastes feeds, fodder, and the bedding materials. FYM is the main organic manure used in Nepal.
However, Nepalese farmers have the practice of applying immature (un-decomposed) FYM in their field. Immature manure FYM can cause the generation of harmful gas ammonia and wilting, and disturb for well germination of seeds. And, also the immature (un-decomposed) manure does not improve soil conditioning capacity of increasing water holding and fertilizer holding ability. Weed seeds can also survive inside manure and as a result, weeds problems occur in the field.
Well decomposed FYM can solve many crop growth problems in the fields.
raw_english_backup:
--- Page 38 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 31
PART 1: Farm Yard Manure (FYM)
1. Introduction
Generally Nepalese farmers have the practice of collecting FYM from their livestock shed
as an organic fertilizer for their crop fields.
Farm Yard Manure is the manure prepared in decomposed from by collecting dung and
urine of farm animals along with wastes feeds, fodder, and the bedding materials. FYM is
the main organic manure used in Nepal.
However, Nepalese farmers have the practice of applying immature (un-decomposed)
FYM in their field. Immature manure FYM can cause the generation of harmful gas
ammonia and wilting, and disturb for well germination of seeds. And, also the immature
(un-decomposed) manure does not improve soil conditioning capacity of increasing water
holding and fertilizer holding ability. Weed seeds can also survive inside manure and as a
result, weeds problems occur in the field.
Well decomposed FYM can solve many crop growth problems in the fields.

title: Advantages and Applications of Well Decomposed Farm Yard Manure (FYM)
summary: This text discusses the benefits of using well decomposed farm yard manure in agriculture, including its effects on soil productivity and nutrient availability.
full_content:
Well decomposed FYM can solve many crop growth problems in the fields.
2. Advantages of Well Decomposed Farm Yard Manure (FYM)
2.1 Improvement of soil productivity
• Application of well decomposed FYM in soil act as a soil conditioner which is capable of improving moisture holding, fertilizers holding and air passing capacity; as well as it helps the capacity of drainage to soil in good balance. This type of soil is very useful for good growth and development of plants.
• By the application of well decomposed FYM, some portions of nutrients are available directly to the plants, and remaining nutrients in soil is released to plants for long time.
• The materials that soak urine should be used as bedding materials in animal shed which can help to minimize the loss of urine. Otherwise, we can also make the urine collecting place separately with plastic or cemented structure, and use urine as fertilizers.
• By using well decomposed FYM in field, fewer amounts of chemical fertilizers are

title: Composition and Benefits of Farmyard Manure (FYM)
summary: This section describes the composition of farmyard manure and its benefits for plant health.
full_content:
--- Page 39 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 32
Composition of FYM
Materials Nitrogen Phosphorus Potash (%)
(%) (%) Paddy straw 0.42 0.20 0.45
Cow dung 0.71 0.70 0.74
Pig feces 1.35 1.94 1.05
Source: Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries, Japan 2000
2.2 Healthy for plants
• Well decomposed FYM is free from weed seeds; plant pathogenic Fungi, bacteria & parasites.
• Around temperatures of 55 °C to 65 °C during decomposition, many microorganisms, plant pathogenic fungi, bacteria and parasites are destroyed.
2.3 Good means of breakdown of organic matter
• FYM is the good means of breakdown of organic matter in simpler form
• During decomposition of FYM, high temperature inside is accelerated, which helps to break down structural molecules of bedding materials (straw, weed, grasses of feed, etc.) into simpler nutrient form which can be easily uptake by plant after its application.
3. Material Required for Making FYM
• Animal excreta: Dung, Urine

3. Material Required for Making FYM
• Animal excreta: Dung, Urine
• Bedding materials: Straw, saw dust, dry leaves etc.
4. The Place for Making FYM
Place for collecting and making decomposed FYM should be as followings:
• The nearer place to animal shed
• Elevated & well drained place
• Place of easy care and observation
• It is recommended two places for the preparation of well decomposed FYM manure.

title: Manure Storage and Fermentation Techniques for Maize Farming
summary: Instructions on proper manure storage and fermentation techniques for maize farming to protect micronutrients from sunlight and rain.
full_content:
--- Page 41 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 34
• If possible, storage place for collecting manure should be walled and roofed with stone, wood, or hay, which protects manure from direct sunlight and rain. It is important to protect manure from sunlight and rain to protect micronutrients; otherwise the micronutrients in the manure will be lost.
(C) Actual Procedure for collecting and using FYM
• The fresh dung together with bedding materials should be collected daily from animal sheds to the first storage place up to 2 months properly, and let it remains for next 2 month by covering it with dry leaves, straw or plastic sheet.
• After fulfilling the manure in first storage place, the manures should be collected daily to the next storage place.
• Put the manure in next storage up to 2 months and cover it like as in first place, and let it remains for next two months.
• The manure already becomes fermented in first place up to two months, when we

title: Manure Fermentation and Management for Fertilizer Production
summary: This text describes a method for managing manure fermentation to produce well-decomposed FYM (Farmyard Manure) for agricultural use.
full_content:
• The manure already becomes fermented in first place up to two months, when we
are collecting dung in second place. So, we can apply the manure from first place to the field, and the first place will become empty and we can collect the dung again on first place. This cycle continue on every two month period on first and second places. This way, we can get well fermented FYM.
5. Condition Required for Well Decomposition of FYM
Well decomposition of manure is required for making good FYM. For making well decomposed manure, the following things should be considered well during its preparation:
• After piled up the fresh FYM, moisture content of the FYM should be managed 50-65%. It can be known by squeezing the manure in palm of hand, if it is wet. If
moisture is less, water should be sprayed, and if moisture is high, the manures should
be dried in shadow by removing cover for 1-2 days.

title: Effects and Proper Handling of Fresh Farmyard Manure (FYM) Temperature
summary: Fresh farmyard manure (FYM) temperature can rise to 70 degrees Celsius within 2-3 weeks after deposition, which is not ideal for proper decomposition. Turning over should be done at temperatures below 60°C.
full_content:
• The fresh FYM temperature rises up to 70 degrees Celsius within 2 to 3 weeks (1
week in summer) after deposition, which is not good for well decomposition of FYM.
Therefore, we should be careful for turning over at every 60 ºC for two to three times.
It can be known by putting hand inside the manure and feel hot.

title: Compost Manure Preparation Techniques for Maize Farming
summary: This section describes techniques for preparing compost manure as an alternative to FYM in maize farming.
full_content:
--- Page 44 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 37
PART 2: Compost Manure
8. Introduction
• Compost manure can be prepared as an alternative to FYM.
• Compost making is the best option for farmers who do not rear animals or cattle with them.
• Compost manure is a decomposed mixture of straws, grasses, fodder, ash, leaves and other parts of trees, farm waste, kitchen waste, and similar material, together with dung if possible.
• Compost manure is generally prepared in a pit or heap by making different layers of raw materials covered with mud or plastic. A wooden stake or bamboo is placed in the middle for bacterial development and decomposition.
• The temperature inside the compost should feel hot. When we put our hand into the compost, it reaches 50-60 ºC at that time.
• Emulsifying concentrate (E. M.) should be sprayed on each layer of grasses or straws or other raw materials during preparation for quick decomposition.

Maize Farming Techniques Manual 37
PART 2: Compost Manure
8. Introduction
• Compost manure can be prepared as alternative to FYM.
• Compost making is the best option for the farmers who don’t rear the animals or
cattle's with them.
• Compost manure is decomposed mixture of straws, grasses, fodder, ash, leaves and
other parts of trees, farm waste, kitchen waste and similar material, together with
dung if possible.
• Compost manure is generally prepared in pit or heap by making different layers of
raw materials covering with mud or plastic. One wooden stake or bamboo is put in the
middle for well development of bacteria and to be decomposed.
• The temperature inside the manure should be felt hot. While we put our hand inside
the manures, the temperature is up to 50-60 ºC at that time.
• Emulsifying concentrate (E. M.) should be sprayed in each layer of the grasses or
straw or other raw materials during the preparation of manure for quick
decomposition of the manure.

title: Advantages of Compost Manure for Soil Improvement
summary: Discusses the benefits of compost manure in improving soil fertility and quality.
full_content:
decomposition of the manure.
9. The Advantages of the Compost Manure
• It provides necessary nutrients to the soil for improving its fertility.
• Increases the water absorbing capacity of soil.
• Improves the physical, chemical and biological quality of soil.
• Increase air aeration and water movement in the soil.
• Manure can be kept in soil for long time.
• Increases the microbial activity in the soil; due to which soil become soft, and
increase fertility status in the soil.
• Compost manure can be prepared in own accessible places, so that it will be easy for carrying manure in the field.

9. The Advantages of the Compost Manure
• It provides necessary nutrients to the soil for improving its fertility.
• Increases the water absorbing capacity of soil.
• Improves the physical, chemical and biological quality of soil.
• Increase air aeration and water movement in the soil.
• Manure can be kept in soil for long time.
• Increases the microbial activity in the soil; due to which soil become soft, and
increase fertility status in the soil.
• Compost manure can be prepared in own accessible places, so that it will be easy for
carrying manure in the field.

title: Compost Manure Techniques for Maize Farming
summary: Instructions and methods for preparing compost manure for maize farming including materials required, place selection, and preparation techniques.
full_content:
--- Page 45 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 38
10. The Materials Required for Making Compost Manure
• Waste straws and grasses.
• Weeds, leaves fallen from trees.
• Roots and stems of young plants.
• Dung and urine of animals.
• Organic kitchen product (vegetable, food wastes)
• Limestone, ash, urea, etc.
11. The Place for Making Compost Manure
• The near place from farm.
• Elevated & well drained place
• Place of easy care and observation.
12. Methods of Preparing Compost Manure
12.1 Methods of preparing compost in pit
(A) Digging pit
• It is better to dig a pit on winter season than in summer season due to which the chance of drying out of material is less.
• The length of the pit should be as per need, but depth should not be more than one meter.
(B) Filling pit
• The collected raw materials for making compost manure should be kept arranging in layers inside the pit.
• Wooden stick or a bamboo should be put in the middle of the pit during filling materials inside the pit.

• Wooden stick or a bamboo should be put in the middle of the pit during filling

title: Compost Preparation Techniques
summary: Instructions for preparing compost manure by layering and watering each layer during the process.
full_content:
• Spraying water in each layer of compost during its preparation will help for
making good compost manure.
• It is better to make each layer up to15-20 cm (1 to 1.5 hand span).

title: Composting Techniques for Maize Farming
summary: Detailed instructions on maize farming techniques including composting methods and materials used.
full_content:
--- Page 46 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 39
• We can also use dried leaves, green fodder, dung, water (2-3 liter of water in each layer), lime powder (100-200 gm. in each layer), E. M. liquid (Mixture of 1 liter E.M. and 10-15 liter water, and spray nearly 1 liter in each layer) and forest soil (2-3 kg. in each layer) between each layer which help to decompose the manure well.
• E.M. liquid, Dung, Urine, lime powder is not mandatory, but it is better to apply for producing well decomposed FYM.
• Lime powder preserves the manure from its acidity and increases the bacterial activities. Likewise, forest soil increases the bacterial numbers.
• The use of dung and urine between the raw materials will increase the quality of the compost.
• The raw materials should be forcefully pressed inside the pit so that it decays soon.
(C) Using bamboo or wood stake for manure ventilation
• During the winter and dry summer, the compost should be prepared by pit method

title: Compost Preparation and Maintenance Techniques
summary: Instructions for preparing and maintaining compost using the pit method, including temperature control and covering techniques.
full_content:
• During the winter and dry summer, the compost should be prepared by pit method
 due to which the moisture can be preserved inside the pit.
• In case the temperature inside the compost become so much hot (over than 70 ºC),
bacteria cannot be survived and that will damage the compost, which will give the
less quality of compost. Therefore, to maintain the temperature only up to 60 ºC,
bamboo or wooden stake should be put during the time of compost preparation.
• The bamboo or wooden stake should be swing or stirred round by hand in every
week for maintaining the temperature and passing air inside the manure, which
will give well decomposed compost manure after 3-4 month.
(D) Covering by mud or plastic
• After filling up the materials the pit should be covered with mud.
• Covering the pit with mud stops the spread of bad smell and preserves manure
from rain, direct sun-light and from nutrient loss.

(D) Covering by mud or plastic
• After filling up the materials the pit should be covered with mud.
• Covering the pit with mud stops the spread of bad smell and preserves manure
from rain, direct sun-light and from nutrient loss.

title: Timing and Identification of Compost Manure Turnover in Maize Farming
summary: Instructions on when to turn over compost manure for maize farming and how to identify well-decomposed compost.
full_content:
--- Page 50 ---
Maize Farming Techniques Manual 43
(E) The timing of turn-over
• Compost manure should be turned over when its temperature becomes near 60 ºC, which can be identified by putting your hand inside and feels very hot. Thus, temperature should be checked every week.
13. Identifying the Well Decomposed Compost & FYM
• The manure is decayed and appears black.
• The used materials lose their original form, and cannot be distinguished.
• The manure does not stick in hand.
• The bad smell of manure is less.
